title: Learning Mask-Aware Offsets: Two-branch Deformable Attention Networks for Inpainting with Masked Region Avoidance
authors: Hyeongseok Oh, Joonki Paik
publish: 2026 IEEE/CVF Winter Conference on Applications of Computer Vision (WACV)
abstract: Image inpainting restores missing regions in a visually plausible way, yet existing methods struggle with irregular holes and complex structures due to fixed kernels and static attention. In this paper, we propose Mask-Aware Deformable Inpainting Network (MADIN), which incorporates mask information for position-aware control. The proposed model employs a Two-branch Offset Estimator that jointly utilizes query features and mask signals to reliably predict reference positions even within masked regions. In addition, Adaptive Offset Range Scaling adjusts offset magnitude based on masking ratio to capture broader context when needed. Experiments on CelebA-HQ and Places2 show that MADIN achieves state-of-the-art results in PSNR, SSIM, LPIPS, and FID, while remaining lightweight and efficient. The code will be released at https://github.com/ohhhyeongsn/MADIN.

title: A Lightweight Transfer Learning Framework for Deepfake Audio Detection using Mel- Spectrograms
authors: Thuraka Sai Jeevan, K. Jai Ram, R. Rajmohan
publish: 2026 International Conference on Electronic Systems and Intelligent Computing (ICESIC)
abstract: The latest development of speech synthesis models and voice conversion models has contributed greatly to the realism of audio deepfakes, posing a grave threat associated with identity fraud, fake news, and computer security. To overcome this difficulty, this paper suggests an audio deepfake detector framework based on deep learning that involves Mel-spectrogram representations and a small convolutional neural network based on transfer learning. The suggested system converts audio signals into time-frequency representation and utilizes a fine-tuned MobileNetV2 model to perform binary classification of fake and legitimate audio samples. An audio preprocessing, model inference, and result visualization application architecture based on Streamlit is a monolithic application architecture. As experimental testing on the York University audio deepfake dataset shows, with an accuracy of 91.92, computationally efficient CNN models can indeed be used in real-time to detect audio deepfakes.

title: Analysing Sentiments by Using Machine Learning and Deep Learning Approaches on Dogri Language
authors: Ritika Bandhral, Sandeep Dubey, Shubhnandan Singh Jamwal
publish: 2026 International Conference on Emerging Systems and Intelligent Computing (ESIC)
abstract: In the digital era, there is very less work on sentiment analysis specially on low resourced Indian languages. Dogri is one such recognized language which still remains less explored by the researchers. It is a morphologically rich language and is spoken by people across J&K and Himachal Pradesh and in some parts of Punjab. Deep learning and neural networks are generally used for NLP applications like detecting emotions, hate speech, text summarization and prediction etc. In this paper, we have used some common machine learning approaches like SVM, Logistic Regression, Random Forest and Naïve Bayes for analysing sentiments. Deep learning approaches which are used in the current research are CNN, RNN and LSTM for analysing the sentiments in Dogri text. After the experimental observation on 1500 sentences, it is observed that the machine learning approaches and deep learning approaches are giving <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{8 2 \%}$</tex> and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{9 2 \%}$</tex> accuracy respectively.

title: ConvKAN-Mixers : A New Deep Learning Architecture for Image Classification
authors: Swajeet Chikhali, Suleman Agasimani, J Nithish, Saideep Nayak, Uday Kulkarni, Arundati Aralimatti
publish: 2026 World Conference on Computational Science and Technology (WcCST)
abstract: Interpretable deep learning models similar to traditional neural networks, including Kolmogorov Arnold Networks (KANs), and mixer models based on KANs, were proposed recently. Unfortunately, contemporary KAN, KAN-Mixers, MLPbased, and transformer-based models reveal poor scalability and reduced performance when performing image classification tasks on a larger scale. This work introduces ConvKAN-Mixers, an innovative hybrid architecture combining KAN-like functional transforms, token mixing layers, and convolutional patch embeddings. To create a valid basis for comparing, all baseline models (MLP, MLP-Mixers, KAN, KAN-Mixers, SineKAN, TSKKAN, ViT-Tiny, ResNet-18) were recreated from scratch and trained under the same experimental conditions, using AdamW optimizer, cosine learning rate scheduling, and uniform data augmentation with the ConvKAN-Mixers model. The proposed ConvKAN-Mixers model is demonstrated to be better than all eight baselines experimentally on four benchmark image classification datasets: CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, FashionMNIST, and Tiny-ImageNet. It achieves the best accuracies on each dataset (90.31 % (CIFAR-10), 65.79 % (CIFAR-100), 94.24 % (FashionMNIST), 49.64 % (Tiny-ImageNet)) and outperforms previous KAN-based approaches in scalability and feature extraction capabilities.

title: A Multilingual AI-Based Conversational Tutor for Language Practice and Grammar Assessment
authors: Sandhiya R, Manasha K, Narmadha G, Genga Lakshmi K
publish: 2026 International Conference on Emerging Systems and Intelligent Computing (ESIC)
abstract: Language learners often face difficulty in achieving fluency due to limited opportunities for real-time conversational practice, inadequate grammar awareness, and lack of pronunciation feedback. Traditional methods largely focus on passive lessons, offering minimal scope for natural interaction or personalized correction, which affects confidence and progress in real-world communication. To address these limitations, an AI-powered language learning platform is proposed and implemented that enables real-time interaction through text and voice across multiple languages. The system integrates speech-to-text and text-to-speech for voice communication and employs AI models to provide instant, context-aware feedback on grammar, sentence structure, and pronunciation. Learners can set proficiency levels, engage in adaptive dialogues on diverse topics, and receive personalized corrections tailored to their performance. Implemented with React.js, Node.js, and Firebase for scalable integration, the platform offers an immersive and effective approach to enhancing language acquisition through interactive practice.

title: Ensemble of Convolutional Neural Networks with Transfer Learning for Remote Sensing Image Scene Classification
authors: Richa Kesharwani, Dhirendra Pandey, Pawan Kumar Chaurasia
publish: 2026 International Conference on Emerging Systems and Intelligent Computing (ESIC)
abstract: Remote sensing Scene classification is a challenging task due to the complex nature of high-resolution data scene data sets. Due to advancement in sensor technology, it has become very easy to collect huge amount of scene data related to the earth surfaces. One major problem in the area of remote sensing is the availability of labeled data. Deep learning techniques are state of art for the scene classification but these methods require huge amount of labeled data. In this work, an ensemble of deep learning method has been proposed which utilize transfer learning to reduce the requirement of labeled data from scene domain. The proposed method achieved 86.40% accuracy which is 2% greater than traditional method. The experimental results show that proposed framework have capability to improve classification accuracy in comparison to standalone deep learning models.

title: HyMamba-G: Hyperbolic Mamba-Based Clinical Gait Analysis for Efficient Occlusion-Robust Neurodegenerative Disease Screening
authors: Muduthanapelli Kiran, Vijaya Chandra Jadala
publish: 2026 World Conference on Computational Science and Technology (WcCST)
abstract: Neuro-degenerative diseases like Parkinson disease should be detected at an early stage but sometimes, some minor motor defects in gait may go unnoticed by the normal means of diagnosis. Even though gait analysis can be performed through video-based methods, the current deep learning models are often susceptible to occlusions, computationally expensive, and ineffective in clinical environments. The presented paper presents a new gait analysis framework, HyMamba-G (Hyperbolic Mamba for Gait-based clinical analysis), which combines the hyperbolic geometric modeling with the efficient Mamba-based state-space architectures. The proposed approach has the advantage of incorporating skeletal representation into the hyperbolic space making it effective to capture the hierarchical nature of the human body focusing on distal joints like the ankles where initial pathological tremors tend to arise. Simultaneously, bidirectional Mamba architecture is a model of temporal gait dynamics with linear computational complexity making it able to efficiently process long sequences. A series of experiments carried out on a group of 149 participants shows that HyMamba-G has an accuracy of 84.6 and a sensitivity of 86.2 and it also remains strong when used in conditions of joint occlusion and frame drop. Moreover, the suggested framework has a frame rate of 88 frames per second, which is much more efficient when compared to Transformer-based baselines, which means that it can be used to conduct gait-based disease screening in real-time and in an automated manner.

title: Mixed Diffusion for 3D Indoor Scene Synthesis
authors: Siyi Hu, Diego Martin Arroyo, Stephanie Debats, Fabian Manhardt, Luca Carlone, Federico Tombari
publish: 2026 IEEE/CVF Winter Conference on Applications of Computer Vision (WACV)
abstract: Generating realistic 3D scenes is an area of growing interest in computer vision and robotics. However, creating high-quality, diverse synthetic 3D content often requires expert intervention, making it costly and complex. Recently, efforts to automate this process with learning techniques, particularly diffusion models, have shown significant improvements in tasks like furniture rearrangement. However, applying diffusion models to floor-conditioned indoor scene synthesis remains under-explored. This task is especially challenging as it requires arranging objects in continuous space while selecting from discrete object categories, posing unique difficulties for conventional diffusion methods. To bridge this gap, we present MiDiffusion, a novel mixed discrete-continuous diffusion model designed to synthesize plausible 3D indoor scenes given a floor plan and pre-arranged objects. We represent a scene layout by a 2D floor plan and a set of objects, each defined by category, location, size, and orientation. Our approach uniquely applies structured corruption across mixed discrete semantic and continuous geometric domains, resulting in a better-conditioned problem for denoising. Evaluated on the 3D-FRONT dataset, MiDiffusion outperforms state-of-the-art autoregressive and diffusion models in floor-conditioned 3D scene synthesis. Additionally, it effectively handles partial object constraints via a corruption-and-masking strategy without task-specific training, demonstrating advantages in scene completion and furniture arrangement tasks.

title: Student Behaviour Analysis in Online Proctoring Using XGBoost
authors: Vijaya Shetty S, Jyoti Neeli, Srujana S N, Tilak S K, Sudeep H M, Thejesh M
publish: 2026 International Conference on Emerging Systems and Intelligent Computing (ESIC)
abstract: The coronavirus pandemic made it clear that trustworthy ways are needed to take exams online for students. This report is about a way to monitor students take exams online using artificial intelligence. The system checks what students are doing during the exam by looking at pictures and videos of them. It uses a tool to look at each frame of the video in real time to see if the student is doing something they should not be doing. This includes things like looking from the screen moving their eyes around trying to trick the system with a fake face talking or making faces having someone else in the room, with them or using things that they are not supposed to have like phones, books or other computers. The data that shows how people behave is put together into organized reports. Then looked at using special computer programs like Random Forest, Decision Tree, Linear Regression and Ridge Regression and Lasso Regression and XGBoost. XGBoost is chosen and implemented using Python and Django. It is private and it runs on its own because it makes the fewest mistakes. It has a low error rate of 4.55. This means it is fast and accurate.

title: Vector: Canonical Intent Graphs and Mode-Aware Execution for State-Persistent Web Interaction
authors: Gautham Venkatesan, B Sharan, P Rama, J. Kalaivani, V. Angayarkanni
publish: 2026 International Conference on Electronic Systems and Intelligent Computing (ICESIC)
abstract: 抓取摘要失败: Unterminated string starting at: line 1 column 3090 (char 3089)

title: A Frequency-Selective Hybrid-Core Architecture for Third-Harmonic Loss Optimization in Wireless Power Transfer Systems
authors: Jiatong Li, Chunsen Tang, Lei zhao, Yuhang Gan, Udaya Madawala
abstract: This letter proposes a frequency-selective hybrid-core architecture to improve the DC-DC efficiency of a 350-kHz WPT system. In this design, the power from the fundamental-frequency is transmitted through a low-frequency core with high permeability magnetics (e.g., PC95), whilist, the third-harmonic power is transmitted through a high-frequency magnetic material (e.g., PC200). By allocating spectral components to magnetic materials according to their frequency characteristics, the proposed hybrid-core configuration reduces third-harmonic-related loss in the main magnetic path and alleviates thermal stress in the magnetic components. A mathematical model has been established to verify the validity of the proposed method. A 10 kW prototype was built alongside a conventional system for comparison. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed approach achieves a peak efficiency of 98.22%, compared with 97.96% for the conventional system. These findings indicate a viable pathway to further efficiency enhancement even when conventional designs are widely regarded as highly optimized.

title: GPRS-Net: Global Statistical Prior Guided Relation-Aware Symmetric Point Cloud Completion
authors: Zelin Li, Shuanfeng Zhao, Zhengheng Li
abstract: Point cloud completion aims to recover geometrically complete and topologically consistent 3D shapes from partial observations. However, existing methods still struggle with thin structures, sharp features, and strongly anisotropic regions, where local discontinuities, point scattering, and blurred boundaries are likely to occur, especially under weak symmetry and complex topology. This issue largely stems from the lack of reliable global shape constraints during symmetry guided coarse skeleton generation, while isotropic neighborhood aggregation tends to introduce cross part interference and feature confusion in anisotropic areas. To address this problem, we propose a Global Statistical Prior guided Relation Aware Symmetric Network (GPRS Net) for point cloud completion. The proposed method first introduces a directional projection statistics module to extract global statistical priors from the depth distributions of multidirectional projections of the input point cloud. These priors are fused with keypoint features to guide per keypoint transformation and translation regression, producing a structurally consistent symmetric coarse skeleton. A relation aware edge aggregation encoder is then designed to jointly model scale differences and directional deviations on KNN graphs, enabling adaptive edge feature recalibration and aggregation for stronger local geometric representation. Finally, a two stage progressive refinement backbone is adopted to progressively recover fine details while preserving overall structural consistency. Experiments on PCN and MVP demonstrate consistent improvements, as well as better structural stability and detail continuity in challenging cases.

title: GNNLicense: An Active Intellectual Property Protection Technique for Graph Neural Networks based on Autoencoder
authors: Mingfu Xue, Longlong Hu, Linji Zhang, Yang Shi, Liquan Chen
publish: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology
abstract: Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) are widely utilized in areas such as social network analysis, recommendation systems, and fraud detection. Protecting the intellectual property (IP) of GNN models is crucial to avoid potential economic losses. However, existing researches on GNN IP protection rely on watermarking techniques for passive copyright verification. In this paper, we propose GNNLicense, the first active GNN IP protection and user’s identity management method against GNN piracy. The proposed method employs encoder-decoder models to encode and reconstruct graph features. Since the graph encoded by the encoder can only be decoded by the corresponding decoder, this design achieves active IP protection. In addition, the proposed method leverages fingerprint to manage and distinguish between different authorized users’ identities. The encoder is held by the user, while the decoder is retained by the service provider. The user encodes the input graph data through the encoder, and then submits it to the server for normal predictions. This mechanism makes unauthorized users can only obtain very poor model performance while ensures the normal use of authorized users. Experimental results on five graph datasets and five popular GNN models across node classification and graph classification tasks demonstrate the method’s effectiveness. Moreover, the method maintains its robustness when subjected to fine-tuning and adaptive attacks.

title: Teaching Radio Waves to Compute: Smart Cities will Treat Interference as a New Kind of Calculator
authors: Ana I. Pérez-Neira, Alphan Sahin
abstract: Picture a highway with networked autonomous cars driving along it. On a serene, cloudless day, these cars need only exchange thimblefuls of data with one another. Now picture the same stretch in a sudden snow squall: The cars rapidly need to share vast amounts of essential new data about slippery roads, emergency braking, and changing conditions.

title: Quantitative Controllability Analysis and Optimization in Attitude and Altitude Control of High Dynamic Flight Vehicles
authors: Kai Shen, Yingxin Liu, Ding Zhong, Zheng Xu, Hang Xiao
publish: IEEE Transactions on Aerospace and Electronic Systems
abstract: As an extension of controllability theory, degree of controllability (DOC) gives a deeper insight into the quantitative performance of dynamical systems. In this paper, to explore the engineering application of DOC, a definition of DOC from the energy perspective is introduced to analyze the controller performance of a high dynamic flight vehicle (HDFV). The scenario of altitude and attitude control for HDFV with active disturbance rejection control (ADRC) is modeled. In particular, a quantitative indicator based on DOC is designed and embedded into particle swarm optimization (PSO) method to optimize controller parameters. Comparison between the proposed DOC-based indicator and traditional indicators based on error integration is made by numerical simulations. According to the results, the effectiveness of the proposed DOC PSO approach is verified by satisfied state regulation performance and robust anti-disturbance capability, which behaviors better than the classical PSO method.

title: Nash Equilibrium in Hybrid Impulsive Nonzero-Sum Game: A Weak Convexity Framework
authors: Tong Liu, Bin Hu, Zhi-Hong Guan, Dingxue Zhang, Tao Li
publish: IEEE Transactions on Automation Science and Engineering
abstract: This paper investigates the existence of Nash equilibria in multi-player nonzero-sum differential games governed by hybrid impulsive systems, where one player applies impulse controls while the remaining N players adopt piecewise-continuous controls. The proposed framework captures complex dynamics in which gradual adjustments coexist with abrupt interventions, with applications in traffic management, power dispatch, and emergency response. First, the necessary conditions for the existence of hybrid-strategy Nash equilibrium are rigorously derived using a variational method. Second, under traditional convexity assumptions, a sufficiency theorem for equilibrium existence is provided. By further introducing pseudoconvexity and invexity, a weak convexity framework with broader applicability is proposed, significantly relaxing the regularity requirements on the cost functions. Finally, numerical simulations, including a power system load dispatch case, demonstrate the validity of the theoretical results.

title: DCAChain: A Decentralized Cross-Domain Access Control Architecture with Key Abuse Resistance for the Metaverse
authors: Chien-Ming Chen, Bohao Xiang, Saru Kumari
abstract: The rapid convergence of the Internet of Things and the metaverse has intensified cross-domain data exchange and digital asset sharing, raising new challenges in access control, key abuse prevention, and interoperability across heterogeneous domains. Existing blockchain-based solutions still rely on centralized authorities or isolated single-chain architectures, which limit scalability and flexible cross-domain coordination. To address these issues, this paper proposes DCAChain, a decentralized cross-domain access-control architecture for the metaverse. DCAChain integrates a relay chain–parachain model with hashed timelock contracts to enable verifiable cross-domain coordination, and builds upon accountable CP-ABE/NIZK-based authorization to resist key abuse without trusted intermediaries. The framework further supports dynamic attribute lifecycle management, including expiration, revocation, and key update, and introduces smart-contract-based policy reuse and attribute-set deduplication to reduce repeated encryption redundancy in asynchronous multi-user scenarios. Experimental results on Ethereum and Hyperledger Sawtooth show that DCAChain achieves lower operational cost, reduced latency, and improved scalability compared with representative frameworks. These results indicate that DCAChain is a practical solution for privacy-preserving cross-domain access control in metaverse environments.

title: Distilling Large Language Models for Network Active Queue Management
authors: Shiva Raj Pokhrel, Deol Satish, Jonathan Kua, Anwar Walid
abstract: We propose AQM-LLM, a framework that distills Large Language Models for Active Queue Management in modern networks. Unlike conventional learning-based AQMs that require extensive feature engineering and struggle with dynamic conditions, our approach leverages the contextual reasoning capability of LLMs to enhance the Low Latency, Low Loss, and Scalable Throughput (L4S) architecture with minimal manual intervention. The L4S-LLM design introduces three key components: (i) a state encoder that transforms heterogeneous network telemetry into token embeddings, (ii) a specialized L4S-LLM head that produces congestion actions in a single inference step, and (iii) a data-driven Low-Rank Adaptation scheme that drastically reduces trainable parameters while preserving accuracy. Our open-source FreeBSD-14 implementation demonstrates improved queue delay stability and higher bandwidth utilization for both DCTCP and UDP Prague traffic. We emphasize that this work demonstrates architectural feasibility through controlled experiments; deployment on router-class hardware will require additional model optimization (e.g., compression, pruning, or quantization) and is left for future work.

title: TRACE: Confounder-free Adversarial Fine-tuning for Robust Object Detection
authors: Wonho Lee, Jisu Lee, Hyunsik Na, Sohee Park, Daeseon Choi
publish: 2026 IEEE/CVF Winter Conference on Applications of Computer Vision (WACV)
abstract: Adversarial patch attacks critically endanger object detection systems by causing severe mispredictions with small, easily realizable perturbations in both digital and physical environments. Existing defenses such as certified methods or patch detection suffer from high latency, while conventional adversarial training often overfits to specific patches and lacks generalization, particularly in multi-object scenarios. To overcome high latency and poor generalization, we introduce TRACE (Tuning Robustness by Adversarial-patch Confounder Elimination), an adversarial fine-tuning framework that leverages Instrumental Variable Regression in the feature space. TRACE treats patch-related variations—including location, rotation, and brightness—as confounders, thereby eliminating spurious correlations and guiding the model toward causal features that sustain robust detection. Evaluations on YOLOv5 and YOLOv8 show that TRACE consistently outperforms conventional defense methods in both efficiency and robustness under adaptive and unseen patch attacks. Moreover, physical testbed experiments confirm its effectiveness beyond digital settings, highlighting TRACE as a practical solution for achieving generalized robustness in object detection.

title: 28 GHz Indoor Continuous-Space Channel Measurements and AI-Enabled 6G Channel Map Construction
authors: Tianrun Qi, Cheng-Xiang Wang, Chen Huang, Junling Li, Xiping Wu, John Thompson
abstract: Conventional wireless channel measurements and modeling typically study channels via discrete spatial sampling. As the sixth-generation (6G) wireless communication places higher demands on the accuracy of channel state information, this discrete approximation becomes insufficient and motivates research on continuous-space channels. In this work, 28 GHz indoor continuous-space channel measurements are conducted, and key channel characteristics are analyzed. Based on the analysis, the necessity of continuous-space channel research is validated, and the role of channel maps is demonstrated. Furthermore, a continuous-space channel map construction method using the Graph SAmple and aggreGatE (GraphSAGE) algorithm is proposed. By the learning on spatial aggregation function rather than performing a passive weighted sum, the proposed GraphSAGE-based map construction method can reduce the performance bias caused by discrete spatial sampling and recover the continuous-space channels accurately. Continuous-space measurements are used as benchmarks to compare the performance of the GraphSAGE-based channel map. Extensive experiments confirm the superiority of the proposed GraphSAGE-based method over existing artificial intelligence (AI) algorithms, providing a robust approach for the 6G continuous-space channel map construction.

title: Machine Learning Assisted Tilt/Azimuth Estimation for Passive Stylus Pens using Position-Based Model Selection
authors: Junseo Jo, Hyungcheol Shin, Youngjoo Lee
abstract: This paper presents an advanced machine learning (ML) model to calculate the tilt and azimuth angles of passive stylus pens on display panels. Based on real-world sensing data, preprocessing steps were developed to automatically capture data windows centered on the peak-value positions, and a custom convolutional neural network (CNN) was applied to estimate the tilt/azimuth information in detail. Considering the missing data samples in edge areas, novel training and inference procedures are newly introduced by adopting the position-based model selection, successfully increasing the estimation quality by assigning the dedicated CNN model per input data window. Compared to the conventional approaches, experimental results reveal that our optimization methods lead to an attractive ML-based tilt/azimuth calculation by significantly reducing the estimation errors when the input position belongs to edge regions.

title: GBFRS: Robust Fuzzy Rough Sets via Granular Ball Computing
authors: Xiaoyu Lian, Shuyin Xia, Binbin Sang, Guoyin Wang, Xinbo Gao
publish: IEEE Transactions on Neural Networks and Learning Systems
abstract: Fuzzy rough set (FRS) theory is effective for handling uncertainty and vagueness in complex datasets. However, most existing models rely on fine-grained pointwise analysis, resulting in poor robustness to noise. This article integrates granular ball computing into FRSs by replacing individual data points with granular balls of varying sizes. A granular ball FRS (GBFRS) framework is proposed and applied to feature selection for the first time. Each granular ball is labeled by the majority class of its internal samples, reducing the impact of noisy instances and improving noise tolerance. Within this framework, a weighted fuzzy dependency function is redefined based on fixed <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://github.com/lianxiaoyu724/GBFRS</uri> structures, where the weight is determined by the proportion of samples within each granular ball. Larger balls have higher fuzzy dependency values and thus receive greater weights, enabling a more stable evaluation of attribute importance. The theoretical foundations, including properties of lower and upper approximations and the convergence of dependency, are formally established. The experimental results on multiple UCI datasets demonstrate that GBFRS outperforms existing methods in classification accuracy. The source codes and datasets are both available on the public link: <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://github.com/lianxiaoyu724/GBFRS</uri>

title: Set-Based Asynchronous State Estimation for Networked Switched Neural Networks
authors: Weizhong Chen, Haoxu Wang, Xiaohua Wang, Ying Zhao, Xudong Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Systems, Man, and Cybernetics: Systems
abstract: This study addresses the zonotopic interval estimation for a class of discrete-time switched neural networks (NNs) under an event-triggered mechanism. Unlike the existing studies, it adopts zonotopic set-membership estimation and considers general asynchronism, thereby enhancing the accuracy and applicability of the estimation results. First, by constructing appropriate observer-mode-dependent Lyapunov functions, certain sufficient conditions are established to guarantee the stability and <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${\ell }_{\infty }$</tex-math> </inline-formula> performance of the augmented error system. Based on these conditions, we present a co-design approach for the switching signals and event-triggered nonlinear observer. Furthermore, a time-varying state zonotope is established, and the corresponding estimated bounds are derived. Finally, the efficacy of the proposed interval estimation approach is validated through two illustrative examples.

title: A Complementary Positive Feedback-Assisted Current Mirror-Based Level Shifter for Energy-Efficient Level Conversion for Wide Voltage Ranges
authors: Shin Han, Gicheol Shin, Jongmin Lee, Yoonmyung Lee
abstract: A complementary positive feedback-assisted current mirror-based level shifter (CPFLS) is proposed to reliably convert subthreshold signals to higher voltages. By adopting a positive feedback structure, the CPFLS mitigates the delay degradation and short-circuit current issues inherent in a prior art, WCMLS, due to its negative feedback design. Additionally, the CPFLS addresses the instability caused by the dynamic node in the CMLS-LED by incorporating a current-limiting circuit. Moreover, the CPFLS effectively prevents the delay degradation observed in diode-MOSFET insertion-type LSs with split-controlled inverter, when the voltage difference between the input and output is small, leveraging its positive feedback structure to maintain fast and stable operation. The measurement results from a test chip fabricated in 28-nm CMOS technology demonstrate that the CPFLS successfully converts a 0.28-V input signal to a 1.0-V nominal voltage with stable operation, achieving an 89% improvement in the measured delay compared to WCMLS. Furthermore, the CPFLS achieves up to 53%/75% faster measured delay and 63%/89% better energy-delay product (EDP) than the split-level converter (SLC) at V<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${}_{\mathrm {DDH}} = 1.0$</tex-math> </inline-formula>/0.7 V, respectively.

title: Beyond Real Weights: Hypercomplex Representations for Stable Quantization
authors: Jawad Ibn Ahad, Maisha Rahman, Amrijit Biswas, Muhammad Rafsan Kabir, Robin Krambroeckers, Sifat Momen, Nabeel Mohammed, Shafin Rahman
publish: 2026 IEEE/CVF Winter Conference on Applications of Computer Vision (WACV)
abstract: Multimodal language models (MLLMs) demand immense parameter capacity to align high-dimensional visual features with linguistic representations, making them computationally heavy and difficult to deploy efficiently. We introduce a progressive reparameterization strategy that physically compresses these models by progressively replacing dense feed-forward network blocks with compact Parameterized Hypercomplex Multiplication (PHM) layers. A residual interpolation schedule, together with lightweight reconstruction and knowledge distillation losses, ensures that the PHM modules inherit the functional behavior of their dense counterparts throughout training. This transition yields substantial parameter and FLOP reductions while preserving strong multimodal alignment, leading to faster inference without degrading output quality. We evaluate the approach on vision–language models (VLMs). Our approach preserves strong performance comparable with base VLMs, while delivering substantial reductions in model size and inference latency. Progressive PHM substitution thus offers an architecture-compatible path toward more efficient multimodal reasoning and complements existing low-bit quantization techniques. Codes are available at https://github.com/milab-nsu/PHM.

title: RSMA-Assisted Edge Collaborative Inference for Internet of Vehicles
authors: Zhijian Lin, Shaowei Yang, Xiaopei Chen, Daxin Tian, Jiguang He, Yi Fang, Wenhao Wu
abstract: With the growing deployment of intelligent applications in the Internet of Vehicles (IoV), collaborative deep neural network (DNN) inference across edge nodes has become essential for satisfying real-time processing and service demands. As a key enabler for 6G networks, rate-splitting multiple access (RSMA) offers improved transmission performance to meet the diverse requirements of multi-user communications. In this work, we propose an RSMA-assisted edge co-inference framework for distributed DNN task offloading in the IoV, where DNN inference tasks are partitioned and offloaded across roadside unit (RSU) and vehicles in a collaborative manner. The objective is to minimize the total task completion time for all vehicles by jointly optimizing the DNN partitioning and offloading strategy, RSMA power allocation, and computing resource allocation. The problem is inherently non-convex due to the strong coupling among communication, computation, and model partitioning variables. To address this, we develop a unified optimization framework and decompose it into three coordinated sub-problems. A triple-iteration joint optimization algorithm is formulated, combining successive convex approximation (SCA) based RSMA power control, genetic algorithm based DNN task scheduling, and Lagrange multiplier method based computing resource allocation at RSU. Simulation results demonstrate that, compared with baseline schemes, the proposed framework can reduce inference latency by up to <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$20\%$</tex-math></inline-formula>.

title: Integrating Pseudo-Supervision and Spatial Constraints for Efficient Clustering of Multimodal Remote Sensing Data
authors: Zhe Cao, Tao Zhang, Zihua Zhao, Haonan Xin, Rong Wang, Feiping Nie
publish: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology
abstract: Multimodal remote sensing (RS) clustering synergistically integrates multi-dimensional information, effectively addressing the representational limitations of single-modality data. This integration offers essential technical support for fine-grained recognition and accurate interpretation of ground objects in complex scenarios. However, existing methods still face several challenges, including insufficient utilization of spatial information, limited ability to extract consistent information due to inter-modal heterogeneity, and low efficiency when handling large-scale and complex datasets. To address these issues, we propose an Integrating Pseudo-Supervision and Spatial Constraints for Efficient Clustering (PSSC) of Multimodal Remote Sensing Data model. The proposed method begins by constructing spatial bipartite graphs from multimodal data to fully exploit spatial information while reducing computational complexity. These graphs are then stacked into a third-order tensor, upon which a robust denoised representation is learned to suppress noise and preserve the core structural characteristics of the multimodal inputs. Based on this clean tensor, PSSC captures cross-modal consistency by minimizing the tensor nuclear norm within the low-rank space. To further enhance clustering efficiency and accuracy, a region homogeneity-constrained rapid label generation strategy is proposed, which leverages high-confidence pseudo-supervision information from homogeneous regions to iteratively refine clustering labels, thereby significantly reducing computational overhead. Extensive experiments on real-world multimodal datasets validate the effectiveness and superior performance of the proposed method.

title: Flight Dynamics to Sensing Modalities: Exploiting Drone Ground Effect for Accurate Edge Detection
authors: Chenyu Zhao, Jingao Xu, Ciyu Ruan, Haoyang Wang, Shengbo Wang, Jiaqi Li, Jirong Zha, Weijie Hong, Zheng Yang, Yunhao Liu, Xiao-Ping Zhang, Xinlei Chen
abstract: Drone-based rapid and accurate environmental edge detection is highly advantageous for tasks such as disaster relief and autonomous navigation. Current methods, using radars or cameras, raise deployment costs and burden lightweight drones with high computational demands. In this paper, we propose AirTouch, a system that transforms the ground effect from a stability “foe” in traditional flight control views, into a “friend” for accurate and efficient edge detection. Our key insight is that analyzing drone basic attitude sensor readings and flight commands allows us to detect ground effect changes. Such changes typically indicate the drone flying over a boundary of two materials, making this information valuable for edge detection. We approach this insight through theoretical analysis, algorithm design, and implementation, fully leveraging the ground effect as a new sensing modality without compromising drone flight stability, thereby achieving accurate and efficient scene edge detection. We also compare this new sensing modality with vision-based methods to clarify its exclusive advantages in resource efficiency and detection capability. Extensive evaluations demonstrate that our system achieves a high detection accuracy with mean detection distance errors of 0.051m, outperforming the baseline method performance by 86%. With such detection performance, our system requires only 43 mW power consumption, contributing to this new sensing modality for low-cost and highly efficient edge detection.

title: Large-Frequency-Ratio Dual-Band Shared-Aperture Conformal Transmit-Reflect-Array Antenna with Independent Beam Control for UAV-Based Space-Air-Ground Emergency Communication Networks
authors: Chuanfei Ding, Xiang Gao, Xiangyuan Bu, Jianping An
publish: IEEE Transactions on Antennas and Propagation
abstract: This paper proposes a large-frequency-ratio (140 GHz : 35 GHz) dual-band shared-aperture conformal transmit-reflect-array antenna (CTRA) for unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV)-based emergency communication applications. A 3×3 cascaded multilayer frequency selective surface (FSS) is designed as the low-frequency (35 GHz) ground plane while also enabling transmission of high-frequency waves (140 GHz). Subsequently, a radius-limited double-split ring is placed underneath the cascaded FSS layers to form the transmissive element (TE, defined as Unit-1). In addition, a miniaturized multilayer FSS unit is proposed as another TE (defined as Unit-2) at 140-GHz band and arranged around Unit-1 to form a full ground plane for the split ring, constituting a complete reflective element at 35-GHz band. Independent modulation of the reflection/transmission coefficients can be achieved by rotating the split ring and tuning the TE dimensions, respectively. Moreover, the joint operation of the two TEs can effectively suppress the elevated sidelobe levels caused by the larger frequency ratio. To validate the design, a prototype of the whole CTRA architecture was fabricated and measured. Experimental results have indicated the proposed CTRA exhibits a peak transmitted gain of 34.3 dBi at the 140-GHz band and a peak reflected gain of 22.8 dBi at the 35-GHz band, which demonstrates its significant potential for UAV-based space-air-ground emergency communication networks.

title: YOLO-SAE: Spatial-Adaptive Attention Enhancement for Small-Object Detection With Quantified Accuracy-Efficiency Trade-offs
authors: Syam Widiyanto, Hsiu-Ming Wu
abstract: Small-object detection in aerial imagery remains challenging due to background clutter, occlusion, and extreme scale variation. This paper presents YOLO–Spatial Attention Enhancement (YOLO-SAE), an attention-augmented extension of YOLOv8 that integrates two complementary components: (i) a multi-head self-attention (MHSA) block inserted at the P5 stage to incorporate long-range contextual cues at coarse feature resolution, and (ii) a compact pre-head refinement module placed before the detection heads, instantiated as Efficient Channel Attention (ECA) or Shuffle Attention (SA) to recalibrate decision-time features. Under identical training protocols and single-scale evaluation at an input resolution of 640 on VisDrone 2019 and DOTA 2.0 (HBB), the most accuracy optimal configuration (MHSA+SA) improves mAP@0.5 from 0.299 to 0.405 and mAP@ [0.5:0.95] from 0.164 to 0.236 on VisDrone, and from 0.208 to 0.333 and from 0.109 to 0.190 on DOTA, respectively. To ensure computational transparency, we report parameters, GFLOPs, and runtime breakdown (pre-/inference/post-processing) for each variant, highlighting the accuracy–efficiency trade-offs of single and composite attention designs. All experiments were conducted Google Colab using Python 3 on an NVIDIA A100 GPU under the same environment for latency and throughput reporting. We emphasize that the individual attention components are established; our contribution is a deployment-oriented composition and placement strategy with quantified accuracy–efficiency trade-offs under a controlled and reproducible protocol.

title: Dual-Polarized Endfire Phased Array Antenna with Forward Beam Coverage for IoT Devices
authors: Yuepeng Yu, Changjiang Deng, Ruge Yao, Zilin Qiao
abstract: In this paper, a dual-polarized endfire phased array antenna (PAA) based on tilted-beam elements is presented to provide forward beam steering for IoT Devices. Each antenna element comprises two mirror-symmetric modules to generate ±45° polarizations, with their normal directions oriented to the endfire direction. Each module contains two dipoles with unequal lengths, excited by a 1-to-2 feeding network to achieve forward beam tilting. This achievement is helpful to avoid large grating lobes, even with large element spacing of 0.76λ in array design. To further suppress the cross-polarization level at the zenith, a gradient polarization metasurface (GPM) is placed tightly above the elements. It can provide additional polarization for the broadside beam, without influencing the endfire beam. A 1×6 dual-polarized PAA prototype operating in the 4.8 GHz band is fabricated. The measured beam steering range is from 0° to +70° with a 3.8-dB gain fluctuation, exhibiting forward and low-elevation coverage. Within this range, the measured side lobe level is below -7.9 dB, the cross-polarization level is below -9.7 dB, and dual polarizations are consistently maintained.

title: Dynamic Analysis and Vibration Reduction of HTS Maglev Vehicle Using MRD Semi-active Control
authors: Yuchen He, Xucheng Zhou, Haitao Li, Xianhong Hu, Mingxuan Yin, Zigang Deng, Gino D'Ovidio
abstract: The limited intrinsic damping characteristics of the high-temperature superconducting (HTS) pinning maglev system pose challenges to effective vehicle vibration reduction under external disturbances such as permanent magnetic guideway (PMG) irregularities. This paper incorporates a magnetorheological damper (MRD) into the secondary suspension of HTS pinning maglev vehicles for semi-active vibration control. First, an MRD dynamic model considering the influence of current is presented, and a vehicle dynamic model developed based on an existing HTS pinning maglev test line and HTS-PMG relationship obtained from experiments. Subsequently, a semi-active vibration reduction co-simulation model is constructed using the skyhook (SH) linear continuous control strategy to evaluate the vertical and lateral dynamic performance of the car body under different operating speeds influenced by PMG irregularities. The results indicate that the semi-active damping strategy based on MRD ensures safe operation while effectively reducing carbody vibration, achieving over 50% vibration reduction rate at higher speeds, demonstrating its clear potential for improving the dynamic performance of HTS pinning maglev vehicles in practical engineering.

title: Hyperbolic Micro-Expression Recognition
authors: Zixi Chen, Boxuan Hu, Junlin Hu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Biometrics, Behavior, and Identity Science
abstract: Micro-expression recognition is a fine-grained classification task that aims to classify brief, involuntary facial expressions into some basic emotion categories. The primary challenge lies in extracting robust feature representations due to the short duration, low intensity, and significant individual variability of micro-expressions. In this paper, we study micro-expression recognition problem in hyperbolic space, and propose a hyperbolic micro-expression recognition (HypMER) method that exploits vision Transformer architecture and learns a deep distance metric in hyperbolic space. Our HypMER designs a hyperbolic mapping module to project Euclidean features into hyperbolic space, leveraging the unique geometric properties of hyperbolic space to better represent hierarchical data. Additionally, we combine a hyperbolic space-based loss and a Euclidean space-based loss to optimize feature learning by exploiting the strengths of both Euclidean and hyperbolic spaces. Extensive experiments on multiple benchmark datasets show that our HypMER outperforms state-of-the-art methods, achieving notable improvements in unweighted F1-score (UF1) and unweighted average recall (UAR) and verifying its effectiveness.

title: Tournament-Based Index Obfuscation for Secure Space Shift Keying MIMO Systems
authors: Raed Mesleh, Mohammad Abudayah, Saud Althunibat
abstract: A tournament-based index obfuscation framework is proposed and analyzed for space shift keying (SSK) multiple-input multiple-output (MIMO) systems. Algebraic properties of Paley tournaments are exploited to construct deterministic yet nontrivial permutations of the transmit antenna indices. These permutations are refreshed on a per-channel basis through a channel-dependent cyclic shift derived from reciprocity. As a result, a matched index-domain channel is observed at the legitimate receiver, whereas an eavesdropper is forced to operate under a mismatched decoding metric. In contrast to conventional physical-layer security techniques, the proposed framework operates exclusively in the index domain and preserves the inherent energy-efficiency and low-complexity advantages of SSK. In practical SSK implementations where the number of transmit antennas is not a power of two, only a subset of valid antenna indices is used for information mapping, while the remaining antennas participate only in the obfuscation mechanism. Reliability and secrecy are characterized through average bit error rate, mutual information, generalized mutual information, secrecy rate, equivocation, and information leakage. Both passive and active adversarial scenarios are considered. Passive attacks include uninformed mismatched eavesdropping as well as partially correlated eavesdropping channels that emulate spatial proximity and common scattering. Active training-phase attacks, including pilot contamination and forced permutation manipulation, are also examined and mitigated through tournament-structure consistency checks leading to block acceptance/rejection. The proposed cyclic-shift realization is shown to operate effectively when the legitimate nodes observe consistent channel information, while its sensitivity to channel estimation mismatch is also discussed. Numerical results demonstrate that reliable communication is preserved at the legitimate receiver, while a substantial loss in the eavesdropper' achievable rate is induced even under correlated and active attack conditions. For benchmarking purposes, a comparison with artificial-noise based SSK is also included, highlighting the fundamental difference between power-domain and index-domain physical layer security mechanisms.

title: Leakage Current Minimization in Single Phase Grid Connected Solar PV
authors: Midhun Mohan, Sreeraj E S, Srikanth Kumar S. Nagavelly, Suruchi Jain, Javal Praful Gadher, Pradip M. Magar
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: Leakage current or residual current is the current that flows through an undesired path in an electric circuit via ground during normal operations or it is the one which leaks from the power circuit and flows through the ground. The most vital consideration in transformer-less photovoltaic (PV) inverters is the suppression of residual current. Literature has many transformer-less PV inverter topologies, with residual current suppressed by the means of DC or AC side decoupling method and common-mode voltage (CMV) clamping. Both DCdecoupling and AC-decoupling indicate that isolation is offered on the inverter's DC or AC sides respectively, while the voltage is at zero. As a result of fewer switches in the conducting line, AC side decoupling typically has reduced losses. However, due to the influence of circuit resonance and the parasitic junction capacitance of switches, residual current cannot be totally removed using decoupling and modulation approaches. In order to reduce residual current, certain transformer-less topologies employ CMV clamping. Residual current suppression method in a DC-decoupling topology (H5 topology), an AC-decoupling topology (H6 topology) and a novel HERIC derived topology by considering the influence of switches' junction capacitance are presented in this work.

title: Triple Switch Triple Mode ASL DC-DC Converter for PV System
authors: Sushma Korra, Sahendara Kumar, Avneet Kumar
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: Renewable energy sources such as fuel cells and photovoltaic (PV) cells generate power at low DC output voltages. As a result, high-gain DC-DC converters are required to increase these low voltages to appropriate high voltage levels for efficient power transmission and DC microgrid applications. This manuscript proposes a new Triple-Switch Triple-Mode (TSTM) high-gain DC-DC converter, which is designed to enhance reliability and performance for DC microgrid applications. The proposed converter topology also enhances voltage-boosting capabilities in line with the effective reduction of the voltage stress across the semiconductor devices and capacitors in the circuit. To evaluate the performance of the proposed converter design, a 400 W converter operating with an input voltage 25 V and delivering power at output voltage of 200 V at a switching frequency of 50 kHz is simulated using MATLAB.

title: Performance Investigation and Characterization of High Semiconductor Devices using ERSC
authors: Aman Keshari, Mohammad Asif Ali, Dogga Raveendhra, Suneel Raju Pendem, B L Narasimharaju
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: This paper presents an innovative method for dynamic characterization of wide bandgap (WBG) semiconductor devices using an Energy Recirculation and Storage Circuit (ERSC). Traditional Double Pulse Test (DPT) techniques require high-voltage sources, which are often unavailable in laboratory environments. The proposed ERSC-based approach enables highvoltage and high-current testing using a low-power input source by leveraging energy recirculation and modular feedback. Two topologies—single-ended buck/boost and boost/buck ERSCs—are analyzed, with detailed operation in magnetize and charge modes. Theoretical modeling supported by simulation in Simulink and demonstrates effective performance characterization of power switches, achieving up to 14 kW equivalent load testing from a 1.4 kW input. The setup also addresses challenges related to waveform acquisition, switching loss evaluation, and crosstalk effects in WBG devices. The proposed method provides a costeffective and scalable solution for power semiconductor testing.

title: Design and Development of a Dynamic Wireless Charging System Using a Single Inverter with Constant Voltage and Constant Current Charging
authors: R. Nakkeeran, C. Bharatiraja
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: The growing popularity of Electric Vehicles (EVs), there is a growing demand for efficient, safe, and easy-to-use charging systems. This paper presents the design and development of a wireless charging system that uses a single inverter to manage Constant Voltage (CV) and Constant Current (CC) charging modes. The system is based on Dynamic Inductive Power Transfer (D-IPT) technology, which enables contactless electricity transfer. This makes charging more convenient and safer than traditional wired methods. A key advantage of this system is that it uses a single power stage to seamlessly switch between <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$C V$</tex> and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$C C$</tex> modes, helping to reduce capacity, size, cost, and complexity. The design is modular, meaning it can be easily adapted to different charging needs and conditions, even by changing the alignment of the charger with the vehicle. Simulations using ANSYS Maxwell and MATLAB, as well as testing on a 3.3 kW laboratory prototype, demonstrated that the system can achieve efficiencies of over 88%. These results demonstrate that the system works efficiently under real-world conditions and is suitable for dynamic charging applications such as electric vehicles. This research supports the future of wireless charging for EVs by providing a reliable and scalable solution.

title: Hybrid Machine Learning for Uncertainty-Aware Net Load Forecasting in Renewable-Integrated Grids
authors: Yogita Singh, Vaibhavi Awasthi, Jyoti Kumari, Deepak Kumar, G. Lloyds Raja
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: The integration of renewable energy sources (RES) into power grids has increased variability and uncertainty in net load forecasting. A novel hybrid Bayesian Neural NetworkRandom Forest (BNN-RF) model is proposed for short-term net load orecasting in high-RES power systems. The model combines BNNs' probabilistic forecasting capabilities with RFs' feature robustness and interpretability. The model achieves a 1.74 % reduction in root mean square error compared to conventional ANN models, providing reliable uncertainty estimates for grid operators managing RES intermittency. The hybrid approach addresses limitations of individual models, highlighting the potential of hybrid machine learning models for improving forecasting accuracy and operational decision-making in renewable-rich power grids.

title: Economic and Operational Analysis of Virtual Energy Storage in Energy Communities
authors: Vishal Sharma, Shubham Patwari, Satish Sharma, Prerna Jain
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: The recent decline in storage technology costs and the rise of economically-driven energy exchange mechanisms are fostering the growth of energy communities. This paper presents an evolved transactive control framework for shared energy communities featuring independent energy suppliers, retailers, and prosumers. The focus is on a virtual energy storage system (VESS), wherein each supplier has an individual battery. Due to underutilization and high costs, prosumers often cannot fully leverage their battery capacity. By aggregating suppliers' batteries, we create a virtual capacity that exceeds the actual physical capacity. This system optimizes battery usage since not all prosumers charge or discharge simultaneously. The control problem discussed involves the efficient scheduling of energy operations for a collection of prosumers, each with their own demand and generation capabilities, and a group of energy storage suppliers that can store and retrieve excess energy from the prosumers. This paper proposes a centralized system in which a central authority oversees and controls all energy transactions, enhancing the efficiency and sustainability of energy usage in these communities.

title: Cost Effective Reconfigurable Induction Motor Stator Windings for Single Phase Integrated Onboard Charger
authors: Deepak Sai Duvvapu, Barry Venugopal Reddy, Srinvas. K
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: Accelerating electric vehicle (EV) battery charging while ensuring long-range remains a pivotal challenge in advancing sustainable transportation. An Integrated On-Board Charger (IOBC) emerges as a compelling solution by repurposing existing traction drive components as an AC-DC front-end converter during charging. This results in significant reductions in charger size, weight, and overall system cost leaving much space for the battery to increase drive range. However, a key challenge lies in managing uneven winding current distribution, which can induce torque ripple and compromise system performance. This paper presents a modified IOBC topology that effectively mitigates this issue, ensuring balanced current flow across motor windings and equal magnetic stress during the charging process. Notably, the proposed solution requires only minimal hardware reconfiguration, enabling seamless operation in both vehicle-to-grid (V2G) and grid-to-vehicle (G2V) modes. The electromagnetic performance of the proposed architecture is designed and validated through finite element analysis using ANSYS, while steady-state operation and control strategies are verified through detailed simulations in MATLAB/Simulink. The findings highlight the practical viability and efficiency of the proposed IOBC design for next-generation EV applications.

title: Embedded Realization of Deep Learning Based LMPC for PMSM Drives
authors: Akash Patil, Yoshit Tapar, Pramod Ubare, Nirlipta Ranjan Mohanty, D. N. Sonawane
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: In today's time, Model Predictive Control (MPC) has gained attention as a viable alternative to conventional control approaches in the motor control applications due to its capability to handle multi-input, multi-output constraints. However, due to its optimization-based nature, MPC requires significant computational resources, necessitating strong hardware and robust algorithms when implemented on embedded systems. MPC's computational efficiency can be improved by using better optimization solvers or better MPC design, but each has its own set of limitations. This research suggests deep neural network based linear model predictive control (DNN-MPC) as a method to reduce computational burden while maintaining performance characteristics. Additionally, this study demonstrates a systematic process of embedded implementation on low-end microcontrollers. DNN model is trained using data generated through a Monte Carlo method based on the LMPC model. The DNN-MPC attained an accuracy of 97.28%, acquiring all the characteristics of LMPC. Despite this negligible reduction in accuracy, the DNN-based model offers substantial improvements in computational efficiency, making it a viable alternative for real-time applications. The proposed DNN-MPC approach has validated using hardware-in-the-loop (HIL) co-simulation on a low-end STM-32 microcontroller. Furthermore, this study also presents a systematic approach of Embedded code-generation on embedded hardware.

title: Design, Analysis and Control of Multi-Mode Cell Voltage Equalizer Using Fly-Back Converter for Series-Connected Battery String
authors: Abhaya Kumar Sahoo, Keerthi Saladi, Sashwathkumar S, Lizanne Fernandes, V. Sankaranarayanan
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: Conventional direct-cell-to-cell (DC2C) cell voltage equalizers exhibit lower balancing speed, particularly in large battery strings, due to only a single active balancing path at any given time. This sequential charge transfer from high to low voltage cells counterparts continues until voltage uniformity is achieved across all cells, resulting in extended balancing duration. To address the above limitation, this study proposes a multi-mode DC2C cell voltage equalizer designed to enhance balancing speed under various cell voltage imbalance conditions. The proposed architecture employs a single fly-back converter operating in three distinct modes, each tailored to specific imbalance scenarios to optimize energy redistribution. A comprehensive design methodology, operating principle, and control strategy for the multi-mode equalizer are presented, supported by flowchart. Simulation analysis justify that the proposed multimode DC2C equalizer achieves significantly improved voltage balancing performance compared to conventional single-path equalizers, thereby validating its efficacy and suitability for high-performance battery management systems.

title: Design and Evaluation of Carboxymethyl Cellulose-Starch Hybrid Gel Electrolytes Toward Quasi-Solid-State Zinc-Ion Batteries
authors: Sreshtha Ganguly, Dilip Bennada, Sreeraj Puravankara
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: This work presents the development and systematic evaluation of starch-carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC) hybrid gel electrolytes (HGEs) for application in quasi-solid-state zinc-ion batteries (ZIBs). While CMC-based membranes exhibit high electrolyte uptake and ionic conductivity, they suffer from poor mechanical strength, limiting their practical applicability. To overcome this limitation, starch was incorporated at varying concentrations to reinforce the CMC matrix. Comprehensive physicochemical and electrochemical analyses revealed that starch addition significantly influenced both the structural integrity and ionic transport properties. The optimized composition, containing an equal mass ratio of starch and CMC (CS50-50), exhibited the highest tensile strength (35 MPa), moderate electrolyte uptake, and a good ionic conductivity of approximately <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$4.38 \text{mS} \text{cm}^{-1}$</tex>. These findings demonstrate that starch-CMC hybrid membranes are promising, eco-friendly gel electrolytes for flexible and safe aqueous ZIBs.

title: Converter Configuration and Control for Switch Fault Detection and Clearance in PMBLDC Motor Driven EVs
authors: Rahul Arora, Sanjeev Singh, Ujjwal Kumar Kalla, Dheeraj Kumar
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: Electric vehicles (EVs) in low power applications such as two or three wheelers, mostly use permanent magnet brushless DC (PMBLDC) motor. These motors suffer switch fault in the drive converter especially open switch and short switch faults. This paper presents a quick fault detection and isolation of faulty leg while replacing it with a healthy leg. The complete experiment is performed on three leg six switch voltage source inverter, which is commonly used to commutate the phases of PMBLDC motor. The complete logic of switch fault detection, isolation and replacement of faulty leg with healthy leg is realized on MATLAB Simulink platform and verified on experimental hardware setup having a processor microlab box 1201 for a 200V, 3000 rpm, 1 kW PMBLDC motor.

title: Data-Driven Fault Severity Estimation in Permanent Magnet Synchronous Machines
authors: Khushboo Ramawat, Anukriti Sharma, Bharat Singh Rajpurohit
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: Stator inter-turn faults are among the most prevalent and damaging electrical failures in Permanent Magnet Synchronous Machines (PMSMs), necessitating timely detection and accurate severity estimation for reliable operation. This paper presents a data-driven diagnostic framework based on Temporal Convolutional Networks (TCNs) to identify and classify the severity levels of stator faults using raw vibration signals. The proposed TCN architecture is developed to analyze 1D timeseries vibration data collected from three PMSMs with different power ratings (1kW, 1.5kW, and 3kW), under four operating conditions: healthy, low, medium and high fault severities. Comparative analysis with a baseline Convolutional Neural Networks with Gated Recurrent Unit (CNN+GRU) model demonstrates that the TCN achieves superior performance, exceeding 99% classification accuracy while offering faster inference and greater generalization. The results confirm the suitability of TCNs for real-time, scalable, and non-invasive fault monitoring in industrial applications.

title: Intelligent Grasp Planning Using Open-Vocabulary Image Segmentation and Large Language Models
authors: Prithvi Raj Baskar, Bijo Sebastian, Alpana Dubey, Suma Mani Kuriakose
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: Intelligent autonomous grasp planning is a key challenge in real-world deployment of robotic systems. Generating robust grasp require solving multiple challenges such perception, semantic segmentation, motion planning and grasp stability optimization. Over the years, many techniques have been proposed to solve one or more of the above challenges. Recent evolution of end-to-end learning for grasp planning has demonstrated the use of deep learning methods that map sensory data, such as images or point clouds, directly to grasp action. But these approaches require large datasets to train, and often struggle at sim-toreal transfer. Moreover the trained architecture is usually not interpretable and cannot guarantee safety or repeatability, which are important factors in real-world deployments. To address these limitations, we propose a novel grasp planning pipeline that can leverage the inference capability of Large Language Models, along with open vocabulary image segmentation. The depth image segmentation is further processed using classical algorithms that fit primitive shapes to the segmented point cloud data, resulting in stable grasp pose. The overall pipeline results in a repeatable and explainable pipeline for intelligent grasp generation, especially for everyday objects. The proposed pipeline was evaluated on two different scenes consisting everyday objects with varying clutter density. The pipeline was also compared with a data driven model on a set of standard shapes. Results indicate reliable grasp generation for everyday objects as well as comparable performance with the data driven approach on the standard set of objects.

title: 3-Phase Triple-Boosted Multilevel Inverter for Photovoltaic Applications with Seven Levels of Switched Capacitors
authors: Vinayak Kumar, Ruchi Agarwal, Pravindra Kumar
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: A novel 3- <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\phi$</tex> single source triple boost (SSTB-MLI) with <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$3 \times$</tex> voltage boosting capabilities is proposed in this paper for applications involving medium voltage and maximum efficiency. It is designed with the lowest component counts e.g. single DC source, two diodes, only eight switches and two flying capacitors per phase. Without the need for additional circuitry, the used capacitors are capable of self-balancing. The suggested inverters are made up of lower rating switches because of the decreased voltage and current stress. The switches in the architecture are controlled by the level shifted phase disposition pulse width modulation technology (LSPD-PWM). A theoretical study is provided for choosing passive components and assessing power loss. It is also tested under low and high switching frequency also in dynamic condition like modulation index change simulated using the MATLAB/Simulink software.

title: Computational Fluid Dynamics Driven Sensor Optimization for Early Hydrogen Leak Detection Enhancing FCEV Safety
authors: J Nithish, NavinKumar TM, C. Bharatiraja
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: Fuel Cell Electric Vehicles offer sustainable options of transport because it does not have any tailpipe emissions. There is the issue of the flammability of hydrogen, which is very high. The leakage of hydrogen in vehicles is a serious setback that has to be rectified and identified. The traditional methods, like sensors of the hydrogen concentration, the bubble test, and acoustic monitoring, lack the knowledge regarding the dispersion of escaping gas. Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD) simulations will be used in this work to study cases of hydrogen leakages and the best positions to place sensors to give the best outcomes. With the help of CFD modeling, it becomes much easier to understand the dynamics of the movement of the gas, its distribution in different conditions, and detection time, and by using this tool, it is possible to increase the safety of FCEVs. Simulation results show that the leakage can be identified within short simulation times as <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$503 ~\text{ms}, 500 ~\text{ms}, 550 ~\text{ms}$</tex>, and 600 ms, in particular situations. The reaction time of the sensors can be greatly reduced by applying this methodology. Locating the sensors focused with CFD will cover all the possible areas of leakage. This preventive safety strategy will give greater confidence in the use of hydrogen transportation in commerce and widespread usage.

title: Design and Control of a Self-Balanced Quadruple Boost 17-Level Switched Capacitor-Based Inverter
authors: Abdul Nadeem, Yogesh V. Hote
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: A switched capacitor (SC) multilevel inverter produces high-quality, pseudo-sinusoidal stepped voltage waveforms with reduced total harmonic distortion (THD). It offers substantial voltage amplification capabilities, rendering it suitable for a variety of power conversion applications, including industrial systems and renewable energy integration. Multiple voltage levels are produced from a single DC source through the use of capacitors and switching mechanisms, thereby aiding in the reduction of overall system cost and size. A singlephase switched-capacitor (SC) inverter capable of producing seventeen voltage levels is introduced. The topology operates with one DC input source, eleven unidirectional switches, and two bidirectional switches, enabling a quadruple-boost output voltage. The proposed converter utilises parallel charging and series discharging techniques for capacitors, in conjunction with a DC voltage source, to achieve an output voltage four times the input voltage. A modulation strategy based on nearest-level control is adopted to keep the capacitor voltages balanced even when the load varies. Furthermore, the inverter is compared with existing MLI topologies, demonstrating its superior performance. The feasibility and effectiveness of this inverter are verified through MATLAB simulations under static resistive loads, inductive loads, dynamic load conditions, and different modulation index values.
