Consequently, a USR maximization problem is formulated by jointly optimizing the network topology (i.e., service classification and CCN grouping) and the allocation of dedicated and shared resources. However, this problem is challenging due to its distributed optimization nature and the absence of complete channel knowledge. To address this problem efficiently, we propose a multi-agent deep reinforcement learning (MADRL) framework with centralized training and decentralized execution. Simulation results validate the effectiveness of the proposed system design and algorithm development.

title: Conversational Image Generation: Towards Multi-Round Personalized Generation with Multi-Modal Language Models
authors: Haochen Zhang, Animesh Sinha, Felix Juefei-Xu, Haoyu Ma, Kunpeng Li, Zhipeng Fan, Xiaoliang Dai, Tingbo Hou, Peizhao Zhang, Zecheng He
publish: 2026 IEEE/CVF Winter Conference on Applications of Computer Vision (WACV)
abstract: Recent advancements in diffusion models have significantly enhanced personalized image generation, enabling high-fidelity synthesis of human-subject-specific images. However, existing approaches are constrained by the inherent limitations of diffusion models, which lack conversational capabilities, and operate in a single-round setting, restricting user interaction. In this work, we propose a novel framework that integrates multi-modal large language models (MLLMs) for multi-round conversational personalization. To achieve this, we identified a performance bottleneck in the detokenizer of current MLLMs, which struggles to reconstruct fine-grained facial identity details. Thus, we enhance the detokenizer with a personalization-enhaced Diffusion Transformer (DiT). We also introduce a multi-stage instruction fine-tuning strategy to balance face preservation and prompt alignment effectively. To support multi-round generation, we implement a chat-history caching mechanism and construct the first multi-round personalization dataset from video clips. Experimental results demonstrate that our approach achieves state-of-the-art performance among MLLM-based personalization methods. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first work to enable conversational personalization, unlocking new capabilities for MLLMs in personalized image generation.

title: MANTA: Physics-Informed Generalized Underwater Object Tracking
authors: Suhas Srinath, Hemang Jamadagni, Aditya Chandrasekar, Prathosh A P
publish: 2026 IEEE/CVF Winter Conference on Applications of Computer Vision (WACV)
abstract: Underwater object tracking is challenging due to wavelength-dependent attenuation and scattering, which severely distort appearance across depths and water conditions. Existing trackers trained on terrestrial data fail to generalize to these physics-driven degradations. We present MANTA, a physics-informed framework integrating representation learning with tracking design for underwater scenarios. We propose a dual-positive contrastive learning strategy coupling temporal consistency with Beer–Lambert augmentations to yield features robust to both temporal and underwater distortions. We further introduce a multi-stage pipeline augmenting motion-based tracking with a physics-informed secondary association algorithm that integrates geometric consistency and appearance similarity for re-identification under occlusion and drift. To complement standard IoU metrics, we propose Center–Scale Consistency (CSC) and Geometric Alignment Score (GAS) to assess geometric fidelity. Experiments on four underwater benchmarks (WebUOT-1M, UOT32, UTB180, UWCOT220) show that MANTA achieves state-of-the-art performance, improving Success AUC by up to 6%, while ensuring stable long-term generalized underwater tracking and efficient runtime. Code available at https://github.com/Kazedaa/MANTA.

title: Graph Query Networks for Object Detection with Automotive Radar
authors: Loveneet Saini, Hasan Tercan, Tobias Meisen
publish: 2026 IEEE/CVF Winter Conference on Applications of Computer Vision (WACV)
abstract: Object detection with 3D radar is essential for 360<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">○</sup> automotive perception, but radar’s long wavelengths produce sparse and irregular reflections that challenge traditional grid and sequence-based convolutional and transformer detectors. This paper introduces Graph Query Networks (GQN), an attention-based framework that models objects sensed by radar as graphs, to extract individualized relational and contextual features. GQN employs a novel concept of graph queries to dynamically attend over the bird’s-eye view (BEV) space, constructing object-specific graphs processed by two novel modules: EdgeFocus for relational reasoning and DeepContext Pooling for contextual aggregation. On the NuScenes dataset, GQN improves relative mAP by up to +53%, including a +8.2% gain over the strongest prior radar method, while reducing peak graph construction overhead by 80% with moderate FLOPs cost.

title: Reactive Current Profiles of Grid-Forming Converter and Power Plant During Voltage Sags
authors: Fangzhou Zhao, Tianhua Zhu, Yifei Li, Xiongfei Wang
abstract: As an internal voltage source, a grid-forming (GFM) converter is required to deliver a natural response of reactive current to grid voltage variations. However, this behavior differs from the current injection rule in existing grid codes, which were initially defined for grid-following converter. This article first introduces an analytical model quantifying the reactive current profile (RCP) of a GFM converter, defined as the relationship between the reactive current magnitude and voltage deviation. The model highlights the impacts of effective resistance, reactance and QV droop gain on RCP, offering clear insights into its connection and distinction from the k factor specified in existing grid codes. Then, an aggregation approach based on Thévenin equivalence is presented to describe the RCP of a GFM power plant consisting of multiple GFM converter strings. The approach provides a simple and efficient tool to evaluate the RCP at the plant level, considering the control effects and disparities within the power plant. The experiments of a downscaled converter and simulations of a nine-converter GFM power plant are presented for validation.

title: Impact of Vehicular Processing Capacity, Mobility Dynamics and Cooperativeness on R2V Task Offloading Performance In Vehicular Fog Networks
authors: Maurice Khabbaz, Hussein Hijazi, Ryan Atallah, Chadi Assi
abstract: In Vehicular Fog Computing (VFC), the per-vehicle processing capacity and mobility dynamics exert climacteric impacts on the task offloading performance of in-proximity RoadSide Units (RSUs) in terms of critical Quality-of-Service (QoS) metrics (e.g., task admission and Head-of-Line (HoL) blocking probabilities as well as the task expiry probability and mean buffering delay). Precisely, RSU buffer exhaustion, RSUto- Vehicle (R2V) denial of task release and, hence, resulting increases in the average system response time are undesirable but often inevitable whenever incoming vehicles into the RSU's range suffer from limited task processing resources and/or happen to be navigating at high speeds; hence, limiting their capability to successfully process and complete tasks within their relatively short residency within the RSU's coverage range. Existing work have already highlighted the possible occurrence of the abovementioned R2V Task Offloading (R2VTO) shortcomings. However, very few publications have quite marginally quantified the impact of vehicle processing resource availabilities and mobility dynamics on the R2VTO QoS performance. Also, the vehicles' non-cooperativeness has been largely overlooked. This work aims at closing these gaps through the establishment of a theoretical stochastic framework, throughout which the task release feasibility is derived from first principles (as opposed to being exogenously pre-defined in prior work) and the above-mentioned QoS metrics are expressed as a function of the vehicles's processing capacity, mobility characteristics and cooperativeness probability. Extensive simulations are then conducted to verify the correctness, validity and accuracy of the derived expressions and evaluate the R2VTO's performance as a function of each of the considered metrics as well as existing scheduling disciplines.

title: A multi-task learning embedded parameterization method for downward shortwave radiation estimation under cloudy-sky conditions
authors: Xuehua Li, Yunhao Chen, Kangning Li, Xiuyu Liu, Weizhen Ji
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: Downward shortwave radiation (DSR), a key component of the surface energy balance, is commonly estimated using either physics-based methods or machine learning (ML) models. Recently, efforts to integrate the strengths of both approaches have shown great potential for enhancing DSR estimation. However, most existing hybrid methods focus on integrating ML with physics by training ML models on massive datasets simulated by radiative transfer models (RTMs), which is both computationally expensive and time-consuming. To overcome these limitations, we propose a novel hybrid approach that embeds an MTL-based model into a physics-based cloud parameterization method, referred to as the multi-task learning embedded parameterization method (MEPM). By grounding the MTL-based component within a rigorous physical backbone, MEPM requires relatively fewer training samples while preserving strong generalization capability. Simultaneously, the powerful nonlinear mapping capacity of the MTL module optimizes the simulation of complex cloud-induced radiative attenuation processes, resulting in great improvements in overall model accuracy. There were three major findings: (1) MEPM demonstrated good performance under cloudy-sky conditions, achieving a Pearson correlation coefficient of 0.88 and outperforming the original parameterization method; (2) when compared with representative models and mainstream DSR products, MEPM achieved notable improvements, with maximum reductions in relative root mean square error (rRMSE) of up to 23.80%; and (3) MEPM accurately captured the spatial distribution of DSR across a wide range of regions, time periods, and cloud cover conditions. By embedding ML directly into a cloud parameterization method, this study introduced a highly efficient and robust hybrid approach. This work provides valuable insights for advancing DSR estimation and contributes to research on land surface processes, climate change, and solar energy resource utilization.

title: Movable Antenna Enhanced Multiuser Communications with Mutual Coupling
authors: Wei Xu, Lipeng Zhu, Wenyan Ma, An Liu, Rui Zhang
abstract: Movable antenna (MA) systems enhance wireless communication performance by dynamically adjusting antennas’ positions. However, most existing MA studies treat antennas’ mutual coupling as detrimental and neglect it by enforcing interantenna spacing larger than half-wavelength, which may result in model inaccuracy. This letter investigates the effect and potential benefits of mutual coupling in MA arrays for multi-user downlink communications. Specifically, we allow inter-antenna spacing to be less than half-wavelength and establish a mutual coupling-aware system model. The sum-rate maximization problem is formulated by jointly optimizing antennas’ positions and beamforming vectors with the position-dependent antenna mutual coupling matrix considered. To solve this highly non-convex problem, we propose a nested Greedy Search and Gradient Descent with Fractional Programming (GS-GD-FP) algorithm. It employs GS-GD in the outer loop to optimize antennas’ positions, while the Fractional Programming (FP) method is utilized in the inner loop to optimize beamforming vectors and attain the maximum achievable sum-rate for given antennas’ positions. Numerical results demonstrate that the proposed coupling-aware MA array achieves significant sum-rate gains over conventional MA arrays optimized without considering mutual coupling in various scenarios.

title: Model Vectorization and Low Fluctuation Method for Dynamic Wireless Power Transfer
authors: Xiaoning Li, Yuxi Gu, Fei Xu, Xian Zhang
publish: IEEE Journal of Emerging and Selected Topics in Power Electronics
abstract: Dynamic Wireless Power Transfer (DWPT) is a pivotal technology for future mobile electrical energy interaction. However, its widespread adoption is challenged by significant transfer fluctuations induced by spatial and temporal factors. This paper proposes a vectorization method of variables to reconstruct the controlled scalar variables into space vector variables for analyzing fluctuations in both spatial and temporal factors. A vector-based model is further established for the multi-transmitter (Tx)-single-receiver (Rx) DWPT system. To improve the performance, the model is constrained and ultimately simplified into a single-Tx-single-Rx model. Based on this model, a stable output of the Rx is achieved just by maintaining the amplitude of the input voltage vector, which can track the Rx position synchronously. This paper further investigates the impact of the coil design parameters on the electrical parameters, cross‑coupling, fundamental content and offset of coupling coefficient curves, which can impact the model and system performance through the analysis. Compared with two existing advanced methods, the proposed approach demonstrates superior performance in experiments: power fluctuations remain around ±1.2% across all tested positions, and average efficiency exceeds 90%, peaking at 92.3%.

title: Life with an Experimental Brain Implant: Early Users Reveal what the Technology Gives—And what it Takes
authors: Edd Gent
abstract: Scott Imbrie vividly remembers the first time he used a robotic arm to shake someone's hand and felt the robotic limb as if it were his own. “I still get goosebumps when I think about that initial contact,” he says. “It's just unexplainable.” The moment came courtesy of a brain implant: an array of electrodes that let him control a robotic arm and receive tactile sensations back to the brain. ¶ Getting there took decades. In 1985, Imbrie had woken up in the hospital after a car accident with a broken neck and a doctor telling him he'd never use his hands or legs again. His response was an expletive, he says—and a decision. “I'm not going to allow someone to tell me what I can and can't do.” With the determination of a head-strong 22-year-old, Imbrie gradually regained the ability to walk and some limited arm movement. Aware of how unusual his recovery was, the Illinois native wanted to help others in similar situations and began looking for research projects related to spinal cord injuries. For decades, though, he wasn't the right fit, until in 2020 he was finally accepted into a University of Chicago trial.

title: Coupling Structure Optimization and Efficiency Analysis in Ultra-Thin Sapphire Fiber
authors: Jie Xiong, Wen Zhang, Jinlong Li, Guanghui Chen, Lianqing Zhu
abstract: A multi-cascaded coupling technique for Φ30 μm ultra-thin sapphire fibers is proposed and experimentally validated, demonstrating enhanced fundamental mode coupling efficiency and effective suppression of higher-order modes. Based on the overlap integral algorithm for coupling efficiency and phase-matching theory, a multi-cascaded transmission model for mode coupling in sapphire fibers is established. The model analyzes the coupling efficiencies of different modes under varying core diameters and input field conditions, while simulating axial field distribution, normalized fundamental mode power, and transverse output field profiles. An end-face processing method using hollow-core fiber nesting and auxiliary polishing is developed to address the challenges posed by the high hardness, single-crystal structure, and ultra-thin quasi-cylindrical geometry of sapphire fibers. High-quality splicing between heterogeneous fibers is achieved under low-arc misalignment fusion conditions. Experimental results demonstrate that, compared to direct coupling, the proposed technique nearly triples the output optical field intensity and produces a more concentrated energy distribution. This proposal provides a simple and effective solution to the challenge of ensuring high-quality fundamental mode transmission in ultra-thin sapphire fibers.

title: Distributed Safe Frequency Control for Networked Microgrids Using Control Barrier Functions
authors: Zhiyuan Tang, Dongjian Li, Yue Xiang, Hongjun Gao, Youbo Liu, Junyong Liu
abstract: In this letter, we consider the problem of keeping the system frequency of the off-grid networked microgrid system (NMG) within a safe set during the whole control process. The control problem is formulated as a state-dependent quadratic program using control barrier functions (CBFs). By introducing a set of auxiliary variables, the proposed controller can be implemented in a distributed manner while satisfying the same safety constraints at all times. Disturbance observers are also introduced in the control framework to eliminate the impact of external disturbances on safety guarantees established by CBFs.

title: An Enhanced Golden Jackal Optimizer for Multi-Criteria DAG Task Scheduling in a Heterogeneous 6G Air-Ground-Space Integrated Network
authors: Ahmed I. Awad, Marwa M. Khashaba, Mostafa M. Fouda, Zubair Md Fadlullah, Khalid M. Hosny
publish: IEEE Open Journal of the Communications Society
abstract: Next-generation computing paradigms are redefining the 6G ecosystem by enabling distributed intelligence over an integrated Space–Air–Ground Network (SAGIN) continuum. Despite these advances, scheduling complex applications modeled as Directed Acyclic Graphs (DAGs) within this high-fidelity, three-dimensional infrastructure remains an NP-hard challenge, exacerbated by stochastic mobility, intermittent connectivity, and multi-tenancy resource contention. In this study, we present the Enhanced Golden Jackal Optimizer (EGJO). This training-free metaheuristic jointly optimizes four key objectives: execution time (makespan), local energy consumption, probabilistic task reliability, and operational costs under non-linear congestion-aware pricing. To navigate the non-convex fitness landscape and mitigate premature convergence, a stagnation-aware hybrid strategy is introduced that integrates a Frame Shifting operator for global exploration with a load-balancing intensification heuristic. The performance of EGJO is assessed using high-fidelity simulations that incorporate Gauss-Markov mobility models and LEO orbital constraints. Comprehensive evaluations against six metaheuristics (HHO, WOA, GTO, PSO, GA, and standard GJO) and domain-specific state-of-the-art approaches—including Modified HEFT and Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL)—demonstrate that EGJO attains a superior Pareto-optimal trade-off and a broader solution spread. Experimental results reveal that EGJO achieves nearly a 33x reduction in makespan compared to GA on large-scale 97-task workloads and reduces handover penalties to 3.0 s, significantly outperforming the 8.5–15.0 s range reported by baseline algorithms. Overall, EGJO achieves up to a 60.5% reduction in the global cost function, with average improvements of 57.67% in makespan and 62.83% in operational costs, ensuring robust and adaptive scheduling in dynamic 6G computing environments.

title: Adaptive Gait Assistance for Foot Drop Rehabilitation Based on Uncertainty Fusion of Contralateral Limb Information
authors: Kehan Xu, Jun Huo, Yize Zheng, Zixin Chi, Yu Cao, Zhaohui Yang, Jian Huang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Neural Systems and Rehabilitation Engineering
abstract: Foot drop resulting from neurological injury severely compromises mobility and gait stability, yet existing assistive solutions often overlook physiological bilateral coordination and lack adaptability to individual gait variability. This study introduces a novel adaptive gait assistance framework for a soft exosuit that simultaneously aims to restore inter-limb coordination and generate personalized ankle joint assistance. First, we design a contralateral-guided adaptive phase synchronization engine that transforms healthy-limb information into an ideal phase reference for the impaired limb, enabling active correction of gait asymmetry. Second, to reconcile stability and personalization, we propose an uncertainty fusion-based adaptive gait assistance that estimates the real-time confidence of each model, arbitrates and fuses their predictions, and produces assistance trajectories that are both robust and individualized. After demonstrating high predictive accuracy and robustness in validation experiments with healthy subjects, a clinical evaluation involving five individuals with foot drop showed significant improvements in ankle kinematics, walking speed, and gait symmetry. Subjective feedback confirmed superior comfort, balance, and confidence over baseline and preset assistance. These preliminary results suggest that the proposed framework can improve gait kinematics, symmetry, and user-perceived walking performance, and support its potential as a personalized assistance strategy for foot-drop rehabilitation.

title: A Bounded-Update Commitment Protocol (BUCP) for Safe Edge–Cloud Adaptive Control of Networked Industrial Robots
authors: Yong Lin, Yadi Lin
abstract: Edge–cloud robotic systems increasingly rely on cloud-side learning or optimization to update controller parameters online. However, directly committing such updates to real-time industrial manipulators poses severe safety risks: network jitter, packet loss, and time-varying uncertainties can convert an apparently beneficial update into a destabilizing transient. This paper proposes a Bounded-Update Commitment Protocol (BUCP), a safety-gated mechanism for cloud-to-edge parameter commitment. BUCP introduces an edge-resident Safety Gateway that admits a cloud-suggested parameter vector only if it satisfies a verifiable three-layer contract: excitation sufficiency, bounded parameter jump, and predicted performance improvement. The tracking controller is instantiated as an inner-loop Slotine–Li adaptive law, and BUCP further employs a short validation window with automatic rollback to contain post-commit degradation. Under the protocol constraints, the closed-loop signals remain Uniformly Ultimately Bounded (UUB), and the update process is Zeno-free via an enforced minimum dwell time. Experiments on a SIASUN SR50A 6-DOF industrial manipulator deployed with ROS2 under diverse emulated network conditions corroborate the proposed design (servo cycle Tc = 2 ms, 500 Hz). Relative to a 10 Hz periodic update baseline, BUCP reduces the downlink commitment rate by approximately 88% (from 10 Hz to ≈1.0–1.2 Hz in our tests) while preserving near-periodic tracking accuracy in S1 and maintaining bounded degradation under harsher links, demonstrating its value as an auditable safety layer for industrial edge–cloud adaptation. Beyond conventional performance metrics, we report process-level safety indicators—unsafe-update rejection rate, rollback frequency, and recovery time—demonstrating BUCP as a pluggable and auditable safety layer for industrial edge–cloud adaptation.

title: Consumer Responses to Ethical and Privacy Concerns in AI Chatbots: Evidence From Offensive Language, Gender Bias, and Digital Privacy
authors: Jikhan Jeong
publish: IEEE Transactions on Computational Social Systems
abstract: Due to the rapid innovation of artificial intelligence (AI) and big data, AI chatbots have become increasingly influential computational social systems (CSS) in everyday life. However, the expansion of interactions between AI chatbots and consumers has raised significant ethical and privacy concerns. Using data from an online survey, this study empirically examines how consumer concerns about AI ethics and privacy influence their intention to use AI chatbots. These concerns are categorized into three types: 1) offensive language (generated by AI chatbots or users); 2) gender-biased language (from either source); and 3) digital privacy violations. The findings suggest that prior experience with AI chatbots is the most significant positive influence on respondents’ intention to use them. In contrast, all concern variables negatively influence respondents’ intention to use AI chatbots. Moreover, this intention varies depending on the type and source of unethical behavior. Specifically, concerns about user-generated offensive language directed at AI chatbots have a stronger negative effect than concerns about offensive language from AI directed at users. Conversely, concerns about AI-generated gender-biased language toward users have a stronger negative impact than concerns about such language from users toward AI. Finally, respondents express greater sensitivity to AI chatbots’ violations of their own digital privacy than to violations affecting other users.

title: Physics-Guided Deep Neural Networks for Radar-Based UAV Recognition in Different Environments With No Prior in Situ Data
authors: Sean Kearney, Sevgi Z. Gurbuz
publish: IEEE Transactions on Aerospace and Electronic Systems
abstract: Classifying UAV radar micro-Doppler signatures with deep neural networks (DNNs) suffers a degradation in performance when there is a difference in the distribution of training and test data due to the differences in clutter backscatter and signal-to-interference-plus-noise ratio (SINR) of different environments. This is further exacerbated in low SINR samples in which the HERM lines characteristic of UAV blade rotations become only faintly visible. As HERM lines are a critical feature enabling the recognition and discrimination of various types of UAVs, this paper addresses the two-fold challenge of 1) how to accurately synthesize HERM lines in training data for UAV classification, and 2) how to enhance recognition performance in previously unseen, different environments, especially when the SINR is low. Towards these aims, we propose a physics-guided approach in which a semantic segmentation network trained only using low-fidelity CAD-models of UAVs is integrated into the discriminator of a Generative Adversarial Network (GAN) for training data synthesis, and on the front-end of a DNN for classification. Our results show that physics-guided semantic segmentation improves the representation of HERM lines in GAN-synthesized samples and enables improved classification of low SINR samples exhibiting HERM lines when no prior in situ data at that SINR is used during training. Consequently, our approach provides a way of enhancing UAV recognition performance in different operational environments, from which it is often difficult to obtain training data prior to deployment.

title: Toward Sustainable Environmental Intelligence: A Comprehensive Survey of Federated Learning Applications and Technical Challenges
authors: Sara Yarham, Mehran Behjati, Haider A. H. Alobaidy
publish: IEEE Open Journal of the Computer Society
abstract: Environmental monitoring systems increasingly face the challenge of processing vast volumes of distributed, heterogeneous, and sensitive data in real time. Federated Learning (FL), a decentralized machine learning paradigm, offers a compelling solution by enabling collaborative model training across edge devices while preserving data privacy. This paper presents a comprehensive review of recent advancements in FL-driven environmental monitoring across domains such as air quality forecasting, traffic flow optimization, solar energy prediction, water quality assessment, carbon footprint analysis, and disaster response. We analyze how state-of-the-art FL methodologies, including diverse machine and deep learning models, hierarchical and multi-task architectures, semantic communication, and privacy-preserving aggregation, enhance prediction accuracy, communication efficiency, and robustness. However, FL adoption still faces critical challenges including communication overhead, non-IID data, sensor unreliability, and adversarial threats. To address these, we highlight key research directions such as energy-aware FL protocols, scalable and secure aggregation, real-world testbeds, and incentive mechanisms. By bridging insights from AI, wireless networking, and environmental science, this review lays the groundwork for establishing FL as a cornerstone of sustainable, privacy-aware, and intelligent environmental monitoring systems.

title: When Does Quaternion DQN Work? Hamilton Product Inductive Bias for Rotation-Dominant Reinforcement Learning
authors: Masashi Sugimoto
publish: IEEE Systems, Man, and Cybernetics Letters
abstract: Quaternion neural networks (QNNs) encode 3D rotation structure via the Hamilton product, offering a promising inductive bias for reinforcement learning tasks involving orienta-tion control. However, prior work has not systematically characterized when this structural advantage translates to measurable performance gains. We introduce QuatDQN, a deep Q-network whose first layer replaces a standard linear transformation with a quaternion-valued Hamilton product operation. On a drone attitude control task where state inputs are 100% quaternion-structured, a 10-seed campaign (2000 episodes each) reveals that QuatDQN achieves comparable success rate to PlainDQN (68.8%± 8.7% vs 66.2% ± 6.2%) while using 7.5% fewer parameters (4742 vs 5126) and exhibiting substantially more consistent attitude precision (final angular distance 11.3◦ ± 1.5◦ vs 13.6◦ ± 4.0◦; Cohen's d = 0.67, medium–large effect). Conversely, in a 3D maze navigation task where rotation constitutes only 22–50% of the state, the advantage vanishes entirely. Through a systematic ablation study, we further show that quantum-inspired encoding, Munchausen regularization, quaternion normalization, and multi-layer quaternion stacking are all harmful to performance. Notably, QuatDQN exhibits initialization sensitivity— one seed produced a clear outlier (48% vs the 71.1% ± 5.5% of the remaining nine)—adding a fourth condition to the practitioner guidelines: appropriate weight initialization is critical. These results yield four actionable conditions for practitioners: QuatDQN is effective when (1) inputs are algebraically quaternion-structured, (2) rotation is the dominant computation, (3) the quaternion layer is applied as a single, unnormalized transformation without additional encoding, and (4) initialization avoids degenerate quaternion weight configurations. QuatDQN's parameter-efficient design enables deployment on embedded con trollers for human-supervised autonomous systems, contributing to the development of symbiotic human–machine intelligence.

title: Lesion Segmentation of Neonatal Diffusion MRI Under Simulated K-space Undersampling
authors: A. Senih Yildirim, Ayça Pektaş, Büşra Koyun, Yusuf H. Şahin
publish: Proceedings of the 2025 8th Artificial Intelligence and Cloud Computing Conference
abstract: None

title: Improving MPC of Hybrid Energy Storage Systems of DC Microgrids Using Deadbeat Control
authors: Serge Yeghyazarian Tabrizi, Mohammad Saleh Karimzadeh, Pourya Shirinshahrakfard, Venera Nurmanova, Gevork B. Gharehpetian, Mehdi Bagheri
publish: IEEE Journal of Emerging and Selected Topics in Power Electronics
abstract: DC microgrids are increasingly deployed to integrate renewable energy sources and storage systems with high efficiency and flexibility. Advanced control strategies are vital to ensure stable operation and optimal energy management. This paper presents an improved methodology for enhancing the performance of Hybrid Energy Storage Systems (HESS) in DC microgrids using a Deadbeat Model Predictive Control (DBMPC) scheme. The proposed approach combines the fast dynamic response of Deadbeat Control with the optimal prediction capabilities of Model Predictive Control (MPC), achieving faster settling times and reduced computational burden. The methodology is validated through extensive simulations and hardware experiments, comparing the DBMPC with conventional PI and standard MPC controllers. The results demonstrate that the proposed method significantly reduces dynamic errors, shortens settling time, and ensures accurate reference tracking without steady-state error. Hardware validation is conducted using a DC microgrid prototype, confirming superior system stability, improved response time, and effective energy management under various operating conditions. Additionally, the implementation requires only two current sensors and one voltage sensor, significantly lowering hardware costs compared to conventional methods. Owing to its low computational complexity, the proposed DBMPC is well-suited for real-time control of HESS in islanded microgrids, offering a cost-effective and robust solution for advancing renewable energy integration.

title: An Ultrafast Indirect Power Detection Based Short-Circuit Protection Method for GaN HEMTs
authors: Ruirui Chen, Ching-Hsiang Yang, Fred Wang, Hua Bai, Leon M. Tolbert
abstract: This letter proposes an indirect power detection based short-circuit protection (SCP) method for gallium nitride high-electron-mobility transistors (GaN HEMTs). The proposed method requires sensing only v<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">ds</sub> of a GaN HEMT, similar to the widely used desaturation protection method. However, unlike desaturation protection, the proposed method directly monitors v<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">ds</sub> after a predefined delay without a diode, enabling faster short circuit detection and improved noise immunity. Experimental results show that the short-circuit detection time is significantly shorter than that of the desaturation protection. In addition, the proposed method is evaluated over a wide temperature range, from room temperature to cryogenic temperatures, verifying its effectiveness for GaN HEMTs in cryogenic applications.

title: Hyperdimensional Computing Regression: Foundations, Algorithms, and Comparative Review
authors: Toheeb Salahudeen, Ibrahim M. Elfadel, Maher Maalouf
abstract: This article offers a targeted survey and comparative analysis of regression techniques based on hyperdimensional computing (HDC), and investigates how they compare with traditional regression and machine-learning approaches. Hyperdimensional regression encompasses a family of methods that first transform data into high-dimensional vectors and subsequently learn a predictive model within that transformed space. A key observation is that many recent approaches attain competitive performance by stepping away from the original associative algebra of HDC and instead applying iterative linear regression, often of the ridge-regression type, after the encoding step. To disentangle pure predictive performance from hardware-related influences, representative methods are assessed in a software-only environment and compared with standard linear, kernel-based, tree-based, and neural network regressors on benchmark datasets. The review underscores the need for more systematic encoder design, strategies for selecting the hyper-dimensionality, robustness assessments, and a renewed investigation of regression schemes that exploit binding and bundling more fully while still supporting efficient hardware realization.

title: Label-Free Land-Cover Mapping from Airborne LiDAR via SAM Distillation with Convolutional LoRA Adapters
authors: Lama Shaheen, Bader Rasheed, Manuel Mazzara, Dmitriy Vatolin
abstract: Airborne laser scanning supports land-cover mapping with illumination invariance and strong geometric cues, but supervised learning is often constrained by scarce labels and a modality mismatch with natural images. We propose a label-free adaptation recipe that reuses a promptable segmentation foundation model on laser-derived raster composites through a tiny convolutional low-rank adapter while freezing the backbone. We construct three-channel inputs from a digital surface model, slope, and either intensity or local relief, and enforce a strict missing-data convention throughout preprocessing, prompting, training, and evaluation. Pseudo-supervision for adapter distillation is generated from NoData-aware grid prompting on the frozen model; the adapter is distilled from the resulting union masks using a Dice-style overlap loss, cross-entropy, and total-variation regularization, requiring no human annotations. During inference, missing-data–aware prompting and overlap-add feathering produce seam-free mosaics, and unsupervised instance prototypes combined with simple height-based priors map object masks into three land-cover classes (ground, vegetation, building). On the held-out annotated AOIs with polygon reference labels and differing environmental characteristics, the pipeline achieves a mean Macro IoU of 0.568, a mean Micro IoU of 0.687, an overall accuracy of 0.810, and a mean Cohen’s k of 0.599. In addition, we report preprocessing and data-coverage statistics for the broader 30-AOI corpus to make the diversity of the study setting explicit. The paper is positioned as an applicability and engineering study that documents a transparent, parameter-efficient pathway for transferring promptable foundation models to airborne laser-scanning composites without full fine-tuning.

title: Compact Three-in-One Integrated Millimeter-Wave and Microwave Antenna Architecture for Metal-Framed 5G/6G Smartphones Featuring Satellite-Direct-to-Cell Communications
authors: Huan-Chu Huang, Jie Wu, Shuang Cui, Chow-Yen-Desmond Sim
publish: IEEE Open Journal of Antennas and Propagation
abstract: A compact, simple, low-cost, and practical three-in-one architecture, through a hybrid integration of a thin PCB-based FR2 millimeter-wave (mm-wave) antenna array module and a metal-frame-based FR1 microwave antenna, is proposed for the first time for metal-framed 5G and 6G smartphones featuring satellite-direct-to-cell (SDTC) communications. The three-in-one integrated antenna architecture, supporting FR1 cellular/non-cellular, FR2 cellular, and FR1 SDTC functions, is realized by connecting a 1 × 4 mm-wave antenna array module operating in the 5G FR2 band n261 with a maximum scan angle of 45° to the top metal frame of a smartphone, thereby supporting various 5G FR1 bands, such as n1, n2, n5, n7, n13, n20, n25, n26, n29, n30, n34, n38, n39, n40, n41, n65. In addition to covering the listed 5G FR1 and FR2 cellular bands, the proposed integrated antenna architecture can further support multiple 4G long-term evolution (LTE) bands, including B6, B19, B23, B27, B33, B35, B36, and B37, as well as the 2.4-GHz band for WLAN and Bluetooth. Moreover, it is important to note that at 1900 MHz, approximately the center frequency of LTE bands B2 and B25, the radiation pattern from the proposed integrated FR1 microwave antenna is primarily directed upward toward the sky, achieving a high upper-hemisphere power ratio (UHPR) of 70.2%, effectively enhancing SDTC performance and upgrading the competitiveness of smartphones. Thus, the core contribution of this work is to first strategically propose an innovative three-in-one integrated antenna architecture serving as an antenna total solution for smartphones in both current and future generations of mobile communications.

title: State Transfer Graph and its Generating Methods for Place-Timed Petri Nets based Scheduling
authors: Jiazhong Zhou, Jiliang Luo
publish: IEEE Transactions on Automation Science and Engineering
abstract: A state transfer graph and its generation algorithm are introduced to represent the state space of the place-timed Petri net model for a specific discrete event system. For a bounded place-timed Petri net possessing infinite states, it is proven that the state transfer graph comprises a set of finite nodes and an optimal schedule leading the net from an initial state to a target state in the minimal time. However, the size of a state transfer graph grows quite quickly with the net size and the total amount of tokens in the initial marking, which complicates the efficient calculation of an optimal schedule, particularly for real-world systems. To address this issue, two advanced rules are proposed to judge whether a state node is old and cut off when generating a state transfer graph. As a result, two variants of a state transfer graph are obtained, and it is proven that they all contain optimal schedules and are smaller than the state transfer graph, allowing more efficient solutions to scheduling issues. The state transfer graph provides a theoretic foundation for graph search algorithms, such as the Dijkstra, greedy-first, and A* algorithms, for scheduling problems of Petri nets. The proposed approach is demonstrated and verified through a flexible manufacturing benchmark system. The experimental results indicate that the state transfer graphs contain optimal schedules. Additionally, the old-state node judgment rules are effective in significantly reducing the computational burden of graph search algorithms for Petri nets.

title: The Ideal Membership Problem and Abelian Groups
authors: Andrei Bulatov, Akbar Rafiey
abstract: None

title: Gramian Angular Field-Assisted Deep Learning Approach for Fault Identification and Localization in Electrical Power Network
authors: Lidia Ghosh, Sayantan Das, Pranav Rawal, Biswarup Ganguly, Biswanath Chakroborty
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: Accurate as well as timely identification of faults in the electrical power network is of utmost importance for the stoutness and dependability of the electrical grid. In this paper, an efficient deep learning architecture is proposed, integrating Gramian Angular Field (GAF) transformation with Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs), including AlexNet, VGG-16, ResNet-18, and ResNet-34, for fault identification and classification. The GAF induced CNN approach converts one-dimensional three-phase current signals into two-dimensional GAF images, representing the temporal and inter-phase relationships in a spatial form interpretable by CNN models for visual learning. The proposed architecture is tested on simulated data for IEEE-9 and IEEE-14 bus networks with different fault scenarios, including inception angle and fault resistance. The experimental results show high accuracy, F1-score, and robustness, confirming the effectiveness of the proposed GAF-CNN solution for intelligent power system fault diagnosis.

title: Dual-Factor Authentication for Industrial Machinery Access Using RFID and Fingerprint Verification
authors: Harinath Aireddy, Fouzan Muhammad Farook Mulla, Srinivas M, B Kameshwaran, Mohul T S, Namitha Papishetty
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: In industrial settings, unauthorized access to machinery such as lathe machines poses serious risks including safety hazards, equipment damage, and operational downtime. Traditional single-factor authentication systems like RFID or PIN-based methods are vulnerable to credential sharing and misuse. To address these issues, a dual authentication system integrating RFID technology with fingerprint biometric verification was developed to ensure that only authorized personnel possessing both a valid RFID card and a matching fingerprint can operate the machine. Experimental validation demonstrated high reliability with an average authentication time of under two seconds and a false rejection rate below 1 %. The system effectively prevented unauthorized machine access, enhanced accountability through real-time access logging, and safely managed power control via a contactor relay. Designed for easy industrial integration, scalability, and tamper resistance, this system aligns with Industry 4.0 principles by enabling smart, traceable, and secure machine operations.

title: Optimized Machine-Learning Approach for Estimation of State-Of-Charge of Lithium-Ion Batteries for Sustainable Transportation
authors: Zeba Bakhtiyar Anjum, Sheeraz Kirmani, Mohd Rizwan Khalid, Mohd Owais Khan
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: State-of-Charge, (SOC) estimation is crucial for the efficient operation of lithium-ion (Li-ion) batteries in electric vehicles (EVs) and energy storage systems. This study proposes a data-driven SOC estimation algorithm using Random Forest Regression (RFR) and Differential Evolution (DE) optimization. The effectiveness of the model is evaluated using two test cases: (i) voltage and current as input features and (ii) voltage, current, and temperature. The results show significant improvements in estimation accuracy, with <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{R}^{\mathbf{2}}$</tex> values exceeding 0.996 in both cases. The proposed model outperforms traditional methods such as Coulomb Counting, Kalman-Filters, Artificial-NeuralNetworks (ANNs), and Support-Vector-Machines (SVMs), by achieving lower error metrics. In Case I (V, I only), the value of MSE is 0.000065 and 0.000219, RMSE is 0.008045 and 0.014814, and MAE is 0.002168 and 0.004798 for the two datasets. In Case II (V, I, T), the errors are further reduced, achieving an MSE of 0.000029 and 0.000189, an RMSE of 0.005398 and 0.013737, and an MAE of 0.001222 and 0.004237. The inclusion of temperature as an input feature significantly improves accuracy, showing the robustness of the model under varying thermal conditions. The findings highlight the potential of machine learning-based SOC estimation to improve energy efficiency, enhance battery lifespan, and support the development of more reliable electric vehicle and energy storage applications.

title: Hybrid Pulse Power Characterization and Equivalent Circuit Modeling of 18650 NMC Batteries at Low Temperature
authors: Farkhanda Azmi, Mayur Prakash Bonkile, Anandaroop Bhattacharya
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: Lithium-ion batteries operating under extreme conditions undergo accelerated degradation that compromises their performance and safety. This study investigates lithium-ion battery behavior at low-temperature and high pulse current conditions using a first-order equivalent circuit model (ECM). Experimental tests were conducted on three NMC 18650 cells to extract model parameters through Hybrid Pulse Power Characterization (HPPC). The cells underwent capacity testing and HPPC evaluation across <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$20-80 \%$</tex> depth of discharge. ECM parameters were extracted with open-circuit voltage modeled as a linear function of state of charge and ohmic resistance represented by polynomial correlations. Results revealed significant cell-to-cell variations, with one cell exhibited abnormal behavior, indicated by increased ohmic resistance and altered time constants. The ECM was validated with root mean square error (RMSE) below 5 mV, demonstrating high accuracy.

title: A Resilient Controller for Enhancement of Load Frequency Control in Multi-Area Restructured Power System with Nonlinearities
authors: Pranav Prakash Singh, Sarvesh Kumar, Ravi Shankar, S. N. Singh
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: This paper presents an innovative resilient frequency control strategy for a hybrid, restructured dual-area interlinked power system, wherein each area comprises three distinct energy sources: thermal, biogas, and nuclear. This system incorporates authentic non-linearities, including boiler dynamics, Governor Dead Band (GDB), and Generation Rate Constraint (GRC). A novel modified Triple-Degree-of-Freedom Proportional Integral Derivative Filter (3DoF-PIDF) based Linear Active Disturbance Rejection Controller (LADRC) is designed to enhance frequency control of Restructured Power System (RPS). The controller's gain settings are tuned with a modified variant of the Quasi-Oppositional Crayfish Optimization Technique (QO-COT). The effectiveness of the proposed control strategy is evaluated against other established metaheuristic algorithms to illustrate the superiority of the QOCOT. The controller's efficacy is assessed using the IEEE-118 big test bus system. Thorough case evaluations of poolco-based transactions are accomplished to validate the flexibility and resilience of the proposed control technique. This study develops compelling evidence, supporting the robustness and potency of the proposed controller and optimization approach.

title: Analysis of recent TT and QT Asymmetrical Interconnections in BIPV Arrays under Real-World Partial Shading for Urban Energy Networks
authors: Nikhil Arunrao Bodkhe, Debayan Sarkar, Risha Mal
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: Building-integrated photovoltaic (BIPV) and distributed solar PV systems often face significant challenges in achieving the Global Maximum Power Point (GMPP) due to partial shading conditions (PSCs) commonly encountered in urban environments. This research focuses exclusively on the performance evaluation of two recently proposed <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$6 \times 7$</tex> asymmetrical BIPV interconnection schemes-Triple Tied (TT) and Quarter Tied (QT)-under four representative PSCs: (a) Central (C1-CEN), (b) Triangular (C2-TRI), (c) Random (C3RAN), and (d) Right Corner (C4-RCO). The TT and QT arrays are analyzed individually under each shading scenario using MATLAB R2023b simulations, with performance metrics including GMPP, open-circuit voltage (OCV), short-circuit current (SCC), mismatch loss (ML), fill factor (FF), and efficiency <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(\eta)$</tex>. Results show that both TT and QT configurations exhibit strong shade tolerance. The research underscores the potential of these asymmetrical interconnections in improving energy yield and mitigating shading effects in BIPV systems, making them well-suited for deployment in smart buildings and urban microgrid networks.

title: Design and Implementation of HMI by Enhancing Display and Data Logging Capabilities for Improved Process Control
authors: Suhas Gowda S, Suresha C
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: This project focuses on upgrading the PUHP100 Rigid Foam Machine's control interface by replacing the legacy Schneider Proface HMI with a Siemens TP-1200 Comfort Panel, integrated with an S7-1214C PLC via TIA Portal V18. The redesigned system enhances operational efficiency through realtime monitoring, advanced data logging, and structured alarm handling. Communication is established using PROFINET and OPC UA protocols to ensure high-speed, secure, and scalable integration. Simulation testing validated the system's logic, communication reliability, and user interface performance before deployment. The hardware setup adhered to industrial safety standards, ensuring reliable wiring and robust operation. Developed HMI screens enable intuitive control, trend analysis, and recipe management. Operator training and field validation confirmed the system's effectiveness and ease of use. Overall, the solution aligns with Industry 4.0 objectives by enabling future SCADA connectivity and remote diagnostics.

title: Leader-Follower Consensus with Deep Neural Networks
authors: Khushal Chaudhari, Krishnau Nath, Manas Kumar Bera
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: This work presents control algorithms to solve the leader-follower consensus problem of multi-agent system (MAS) with deep neural network (DNN) where leader-follower dynamics are heterogeneous, nonlinear and affected by external disturbances. The DNN architecture approximates unknown nonlinearities with higher accuracy than traditional shallow neural network(NN). A set-theoretic approach employing restricted potential functions is adopted to ensure the boundedness of system trajectories, thereby enhancing robustness against approximation errors and disturbances. The control strategy is developed by analyzing non-smooth Lyapunov stability theory, enabling the derivation of adaptive adjustment strategies for both inner and outer DNN weights. Numerical simulations validate the effectiveness, robustness, and adaptability of the proposed controller.

title: Development of a Real-Time Battery Monitoring System for SoC Estimation Using Arduino
authors: Sumit A. Dudhe, T S R Nrusimha Murali Manohar, Tikam Bhardwaj, V S Kale, Om Rameshwar Kharat, Girish Mittal
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: Keeping track of a lithium-ion battery's State of Charge (SoC) in real time is essential for safety and optimizing energy system performance. In this work, we developed an affordable SoC estimation system using Arduino, equipped with sensors for voltage, current, and temperature. The system continuously collects sensor data and employs a Decision Tree Regression (DTR) machine learning model to estimate the battery's SoC accurately. Users can remotely monitor their battery status via a web-based interface that provides live visualization and real-time updates on battery health and performance. Our setup provides a practical and scalable approach to integrating machine learning (ML) into embedded battery monitoring, making it an excellent solution for research labs, classrooms, and early-stage prototypes of battery management systems (BMS).

title: Impact of Activation Functions and Optimizers on GNN-Based Reduced Optimal Power Flow
authors: Tuyam Tushar Medhi, Sumon Sohail, Sreenu Sreekumar
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: Optimal Power Flow (OPF) determines economic dispatch while considering transmission line constraints. The full OPF is computationally complex as it considers all the line constraints. The computational complexity further increases with system size, limiting real-time applications. The computational complexity can be reduced by considering only critical constraints in OPF (ROPF). The critical constraints are those of the lines that carry power near the limit; other lines can be ignored, as there is a lower chance of violations. Deep learning models like Graphical Neural Networks (GNNs) are used to predict the critical lines. The GNN model offers superior critical line prediction accuracy. However, the performance of the GNN model highly relies on activation functions and hyperparameter optimization techniques. Therefore, this paper analyzes the critical line prediction performance of the GNN model with various activation functions and hyperparameter optimization techniques. The accuracy of the critical line prediction is quantified by estimating false positives (Type 1 errors) and false negatives (Type 2 errors). Further, ROPF is implemented to estimate line rating violations due to simplification and computational complexity. The performance analysis is conducted on the IEEE 73 bus system. Results show that GNN with Parametric Rectified Linear Unit (PReLU) and Adaptive Moment Estimation (Adam) as activation function and hyperparameter optimization technique offers the least Type 2 error and line rating violations. Also, it takes only 53.54% time compared to the full OPE.

title: Steady-State Modeling and Frequency Control for Enhanced Power Stability under Dynamic Bidirectional Load Scheduling
authors: Srikant R. Sabbanwar, Rajshri J. Satputaley, Vijay B. Borghate
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: The Availability Based Tariff (ABT) system was introduced in India to mitigate frequency instability caused by unscheduled power exchanges. While initially designed to ensure disciplined generation and improve grid reliability, the framework has gradually evolved into a profit-centric model. To enforce frequency discipline, the Deviation Settlement Mechanism (DSM) was implemented as a regulatory control scheme that penalizes deviations from scheduled generation or drawl. This paper explores alternative control strategies to address frequency deviations arising from bidirectional load scheduling—an increasingly prevalent condition in thermal power systems. Frequent 15-minute demand shifts place stress on generators, delay turbine responses, and lead to DSM penalties. To overcome this, an Modified Dynamic Voltage Regulator (DVR)-based solution is proposed to stabilize frequency and terminal voltage by modulating excitation instead of relying solely on steam control. A hybrid control approach is presented by integrating a 5-Level Transistor-Clamped H-Bridge (TCHB) Multilevel Inverter (MLI) with a DVR structure to dynamically inject voltage support during transients. The proposed strategy is evaluated under two practical load change scenarios: 500 MW → 450 MW and 450 MW → 475 MW. The results confirm improvements in dynamic response, voltage stability, and reduced DSM impact, contributing to more efficient and resilient grid operation.

title: Adaptive PID Control for MPPT in Solar PV Powered Boost Converter
authors: Heet Dhorajiya, Vinod Kumar Bussa, Tarakanath Kobaku, R Jeyasenthil, Venkata Ramana Kasi, Vinusha Bussa
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: This paper presents a comprehensive study aimed at enhancing the performance of maximum power point tracking (MPPT) in photovoltaic (PV) systems by employing a hybrid approach that integrates adaptive control mechanisms with a boost DC-DC converter. The boost converter is chosen due to its capability to step-up the solar PV voltage with minimum ripple in continuous input current. At the core of this methodology lies an adaptive PID controller, which is tuned in real time using feedback from the PV system. This controller is embedded within the traditional Perturb and Observe (P&O) algorithm to allow dynamic adjustment of PID gains based on instantaneous power variations observed at the output of the PV module. By continuously adapting the control parameters, the system overcomes the limitations of fixed-gain PID controllers, particularly in scenarios involving rapidly changing environmental conditions such as irradiance and temperature. The integration of the adaptive MPPT algorithm with boost converter demonstrates not only improved tracking accuracy and convergence speed but also highlights the flexibility of the control strategy across different converter topologies. Simulation results confirm that <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathrm{P} \& \mathrm{O}$</tex> algorithm with adaptive PID controller significantly outperforms conventional MPPT methods in terms of tracking efficiency, dynamic response, and steady-state error reduction when applied to the boost converter.

title: A New Single Switch Fault Tolerant Multilevel Inverter for RES Applications
authors: Busireddy Hemanth Kumar, A. Immanuel, Moduboina Sreenivasulu, Chinthala Chaithanya, V. Suryanarayanareddy, E. Parimalasundar
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: Multilevel inverters (MLIs) are complicated systems that often have higher fault rates and lower dependability due to the large number of power electronic switches they require. To address these critical issues, this work introduces a new multilevel inverter that incorporates state-of-the-art fault-tolerant (FT) features. The FT MLI design meticulously selects passive components and reduced power switches to achieve a balance between performance and cost. This system is designed to precisely identify and respond to open circuit failures in any of the switches. This cutting-edge technique increases the inverter's longevity and steadiness of performance, which in turn improves overall operation and drastically reduces the likelihood of system malfunctions. MATLAB simulations are performed to examine this inverter's overall performance for single switch faults.

title: Optimal Sizing and Placement of Electric Vehicle Charging Station with the Integration of Renewable Energy Source
authors: Anugraha C, Narayanan K, Anurag Sharma, Tomonobu Senjyu, Alexander Aguila Tellez, Gulshan Sharma
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: The rapid adoption of electric vehicles necessitates the strategic integration of Electric Vehicle Charging Station (EVCS) within existing power distribution networks. This work presents a comprehensive approach for the optimal sizing and placement of EVCS, considering diverse consumer categories - residential, commercial, and industrial. A transportation network was first mapped based on load demand patterns, and subsequently aligned with the electrical distribution network to ensure efficient placement of charging infrastructure. The sizing of EVCS was determined according to consumer-specific energy requirements, while solar-powered Distributed Generators (DGs) were integrated to enhance voltage profile, reduce power loss and reduce dependency on conventional energy sources. Key performance indicators such as Voltage Stability Index (VSI) and total power loss were used to assess the effectiveness of the proposed methodology. Results indicate that the coordinated deployment of EVCS and DGs leads to significant improvements in voltage regulation, power loss reduction, and overall network efficiency. This study demonstrates the technical feasibility and benefits of a demand-driven, renewable-supported EVCS planning framework for modern power distribution systems.

title: Assessing the Impact of EV Integration in Multi-Area Economic Dispatch Under Renewable Uncertainty
authors: Priyanka Hooda, Prem Prakash
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: One of the most significant aspects of power system operation and planning is economic emission dispatch. This paper proposes a two-stage stochastic optimization to evaluate the impact of EV integration on multi-area economic load dispatch (MAED) in a three-area system. In interconnected multi-area networks, optimizing EV participation along with renewable generation significantly enhances tie-line power coordination, reduces operational costs, and supports system reliability under uncertainty. The first stage involves “here-andhow,” determining optimal thermal generation and inter-area power exchanges, while the second stage involves “wait-andsee,” dynamically adjusting for wind power fluctuations and EV charging/discharging patterns using Monte Carlo-based scenario reduction. Simulation results reveal a <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{6. 5 \%}$</tex> reduction in total operating cost and improved tie-line performance through coordinated EV integration. Furthermore, the framework provides real-time dispatch schedules, offering a scalable solution for modern power systems aiming to balance economic efficiency, environmental sustainability, and grid stability.

title: Internet of Things (IoT) Wearable Biosensors and Digital Infrastructure for Children's Health Camps: KasabaKiat Digital system

title: LWFusionFormer: Lightweight Convolution and Transformer Fusion for Accurate and Efficient Depth Estimation_supp1-3689768.mp4
authors: Baoquan Li
abstract: Fusing convolutional neural networks (CNNs) and Trans-former models, recent depth completion methods have shown satisfying performance by effectively capturing both local features and global contextual information. But the fusion patterns for depth completion demand high compu-tational cost and increase model complexity, hindering real-time deployment. This paper proposes a Lightweight CNN-TransFormer Fusion model (LWFusionFormer) for efficient depth completion. The proposed model integrates lightweight convolution and attention mechanism, and the convolution-Transformer fusion strategy is designed to enhance both efficiency and flexibility. By utilizing depth-wise separable convolution, LWFusionFormer significantly reduces floating-point operations (FLOPs) and improves inference speed. The Selective Fusion with Depthwise Separable Convolution (SF-DSC) module is designed to efficiently extract and fuse features, integrating spatial and contextual information through lightweight convolutional operation. Moreover, the hybrid decoder is designed to reduce computational demand, and to enhance model flexibility and practicality. Comparative results show that LWFusionFormer achieves high accuracy with lower computational costs.

title: Design and Implementation of an AI-Powered Mock Interview System
authors: Xin Xiong, Chen Lv, Yihan Zhang, Jia Zou, Peiwen Xiao, Debin Ren, Yitong Wang
publish: Proceedings of the 2026 2nd International Conference on Generative Artificial Intelligence and Digital Media

title: Interactive Residual Domain Adaptation Networks for Partial Transfer Industrial Fault Diagnosis
authors: Gecheng Chen, Zheng Luo, Zeyu Yang, Kai Wang, Xinkai Chen, Jianqiang Li, Chengwen Luo
publish: IEEE Transactions on Automation Science and Engineering
abstract: The partial domain adaptation (PDA) challenge is a prevalent issue in industrial fault diagnosis. Current PDA approaches primarily rely on adversarial learning for domain adaptation and use reweighting strategies to exclude source samples deemed outliers. However, the transferability of features diminishes from general feature extraction layers to higher task-specific layers in adversarial learning-based adaptation modules, leading to significant negative transfer in PDA settings. We term this issue the adaptation-discrimination paradox (ADP). Furthermore, reweighting strategies often suffer from unreliable pseudo-labels, compromising their effectiveness. In this work, we propose a novel PDA framework called Interactive Residual Domain Adaptation Networks (IRDAN), which introduces domain-wise models for each domain to provide a new perspective for the PDA challenge. Each domain-wise model is equipped with a residual domain adaptation (RDA) block to preserve the discriminative structure of each domain and mitigate the ADP. Additionally, we introduce a confident information flow via an interactive learning strategy, training the modules of IRDAN sequentially to avoid cross-interference. We also establish a reliable stopping criterion for selecting the best-performing model, ensuring practical usability in real-world applications. Experiments have demonstrated the superior performance of the proposed IRDAN.

title: A Distributed Braking Scheme for Heavy-Haul Trains Using Coupler Force Compensation
authors: Jilie Zhang, Zhiyong Chen, Ge Guo
abstract: In this paper, a novel distributed braking scheme is proposed for automatic heavy-haul trains equipped with an electronically controlled pneumatic (ECP) braking system. The scheme consists of a coupler force compensator and a cooperative controller. The compensator is designed to counteract the coupler force acting on each car, i.e., the forces from its front and rear adjacent cars. With this compensation, the braking control problem is transformed into a platooning problem of multiple vehicles. The cooperative controller then regulates the velocity and position of adjacent cars. Numerical studies using MATLAB and the Universal Mechanism simulator are conducted to verify the effectiveness and superiority of the proposed scheme.

title: Stacking Multi-Variate Time-Frequency Information: A Deep Learning Approach for IMU-MMG based Hand Gesture Recognition
authors: Sadman Rahman, Md. Mehedi Hasan Bhuiyan, Nasiruddin Mahmud Himel, Rishi Raj Sharma, Kazi Newaj Faisal
abstract: Natural and reliable hand gesture recognition is becoming a key enabler for intuitive human-computer interaction in internet of things (IoT) environments, especially for wearable and assistive systems where camera-based sensing is unsuitable. This paper presents an inertial measurement unit (IMU) based framework that converts raw tri-axial accelerometer and gyroscope signals into structured time-frequency images and classifies them using a lightweight convolutional network. First, we propose a stacking-based multivariate time-frequency representation method termed TFR-StackGrid. In this method, the smoothed pseudo Wigner-Ville distribution (SPWVD) is computed for each of the six IMU-MMG channels. The resulting time-frequency maps are then systematically arranged into a fixed 2×3 kinematic grid. This structured configuration preserves the spatial relationship between translational acceleration and rotational motion, thereby integrating multivariate information into a single stacked time-frequency representation, referred to as S-TFR. A compact Gesture Net architecture is then designed to operate directly on these images, combining stacked 2D convolution blocks with a shallow classification head for 11 dynamic hand gesture classes. We conduct a comprehensive evaluation on the large-scale hand gesture accelerometer and gyroscope Dataset (HGAG-DATA), containing 11 gestures, 23,650 samples from 43 participants, and compare seven different time-frequency schemes, including wavelet and other quadratic distributions. The proposed SPWVD-based TFR-StackGrid with Gesture Net achieves 96.21% micro accuracy and 96.28% macro accuracy, with balanced per-class precision, recall, and F1-scores, while relying only on a single wrist-worn IMU based mechanomyography signals. These results demonstrate that structured time-frequency imaging combined with a lightweight convolutional backbone can effectively mitigate the limitations of template matching, handcrafted feature pipelines, and heavier deep sequence models for practical IMU-MMG-based hand gesture recognition.

title: Learning-Based Multiobjective Coevolutionary Algorithm for Mixed-Model Assembly Line Balancing and Sequencing Problem With Collaborative Robots
authors: Chenyu Zheng, Zixiang Li, Ling Wang, Wanlin Yang, Zikai Zhang, Liping Zhang, Qiuhua Tang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Systems, Man, and Cybernetics: Systems
abstract: Collaborative robots (cobots) are increasingly used to help human workers perform assembly tasks or complete assembly tasks themselves in assembly lines. The ergonomic risks of human workers are a key factor influencing assembly line efficiency. Therefore, this study investigates the mixed-product-model assembly line balancing and sequencing problem (ALBSP) with cobots, considering ergonomic risks in cases where human workers and cobots can operate different tasks in parallel. A mixed-integer programming model is formulated to optimize the makespan and ergonomic risks; this model can solve small-scale instances optimally using the CPLEX solver. A Q-learning-based multiobjective coevolutionary algorithm (QMOCEA) is then developed to handle large-scale instances. This algorithm adopts five vectors for encoding: the task assignment vector handles the task allocation subproblem, the worker allocation vector handles the worker allocation subproblem, the cobot allocation vector handles the cobot allocation subproblem, the process alternative selection vector handles the process alternative selection subproblem, and the product model sequencing vector handles the product model sequencing subproblem. Additionally, this algorithm uses knowledge-based decoding and initialization to obtain high-quality initial solutions. A parameter self-update strategy is proposed to adjust algorithm parameters dynamically. Comparative analysis demonstrates that the proposed method outperforms the original version and exhibits promising performance in comparison with benchmark methods, achieving the highest average hypervolume (HV) ratio of 0.805 and the lowest inverted generational distance (IGD) of 0.028 across 22 instance groups.

title: Rethinking the Role of Vector Quantization for Blind Image Restoration_supp1-3688406.pdf
authors: Jun Xu
abstract: Vector-Quantization (VQ) generative models like VQGAN have been widely employed in blind image restoration (BIR) methods to learn powerful high-quality (HQ) priors. However, the inconsistency between the generation-oriented VQ and the reconstruction-oriented BIR tasks would lead to suboptimal restoration performance, which has rarely been discussed. In this paper, we first investigate the role of VQ in VQ-based BIR methods and demonstrate that the HQ feature extracted from the HQ image achieves much better reconstruction results than the correspondingly VQ-quantized HQ feature. This inspires us to use the HQ feature as a better learning target than the VQ-quantized feature, which potentially improve the restoration of low-quality (LQ) images. For flexible approximation of the HQ feature, we propose a Vector Regression (VR) module to learn regression weights over the HQ codebook, providing more accurate feature representation than the VQ operation. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our VR module consistently improves four VQ-based BIR methods on different BIR tasks. The code and pretrained models will be publicly released.

title: A Broadband Hybrid Rogowski Coil Sensor Based on Comparative Study of Operating Modes for Switching Transient Measurement
authors: Lisong Wang, Fujie Li, Yongliang Han, Yuhang Ma, Zhongqiang Hu, Zhiguang Wang, Ming Liu
abstract: Rogowski coil sensors, characterized by high bandwidth, non-saturating behavior, and non-invasive measurement, have become essential mainstream devices for the development, testing, and application of wide-bandgap semiconductor power devices. The simultaneous optimization of their low-frequency and high-frequency cut-off characteristics requires a systematic engineering approach that regards the coil and integrator as an integrated measurement chain. This paper presents a comparative analysis of three typical operating modes of Rogowski coils—differential mode, self-integrating mode, and transmission line mode—regarding bandwidth, phase response, output voltage, and other key performance indicators. A hybrid Rogowski coil sensor is then proposed, combining the high-output advantage of the differential mode with the high-bandwidth merit of the self-integrating mode, achieving an extended operating bandwidth from 6.8 kHz to 485.5 MHz. Finally, the performance of the sensor prototype is verified through double-pulse tests, which validate its ability to accurately capture the switching current waveforms of SiC MOSFETs.

title: Early Thermal Runaway Warning in Solid-State Batteries: A Dual-Sensing Fusion Framework
authors: Haoyi Yu, Jun Zhao, Ziliang Zhao, Bin Guo, Peiyu Zhang
abstract: As the safety demands of electrified transportation escalate, solid-state batteries (SSBs) are emerging to replace traditional lithium-ion batteries (LIBs), yet their thermal runaway (TR) mechanisms remain poorly understood. Current research on TR warning focuses on LIBs and relies on contact-based electrical methods, most notably electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). Theoretically, the fixed perturbation signal’s repeated calculation within EIS causes information redundancy since only the response varies. Practically, the methods fail at high temperatures due to interfacial impedance heterogeneity in SSBs. To overcome these bottlenecks, this paper proposes a novel dual-stage TR warning framework fusing contact-based electrical and non-contact magnetic sensing. Stage 1 implements online hardware assessing SSBs’ response to a 1 kHz perturbation, extracting novel time-frequency indicators to eliminate computational redundancy, achieving a 9-minute earlier TR warning than conventional assessment. Stage 2 deploys magnetic monitoring for explosion precursors, providing 20-second advance warning.

title: Advanced 3-ω Method for Evaluating Anisotropic Thermal Conductivity in Carbon Nanotubes
authors: T.-W. Chen, M.-H. Liao, C.-W. Yao
publish: IEEE Transactions on Components, Packaging and Manufacturing Technology
abstract: As the limitations of Moore’s law become increasingly apparent, three-dimensional integrated circuits (3-DICs) have emerged as a viable solution to sustain the scaling and performance of semiconductor devices. However, the vertical integration of components in 3-DICs introduces significant thermal management challenges due to increased power density and restricted heat dissipation pathways. Carbon nanotubes (CNTs) have been proposed as a promising alternative to copper for filling through silicon vias (TSVs), owing to their superior thermal conductivity, electrical performance, and mechanical stability. Notably, CNTs exhibit anisotropic thermal transport properties, which can be exploited to enhance heat dissipation efficiency in both the cross-plane and in-plane directions within 3-DIC architectures. Despite their potential, limited research has focused on characterizing the directional thermal behavior of CNT films. In this study, we present a refined measurement technique based on the conventional third harmonic (3-ω) method, specifically designed to quantify the thermal conductivity of CNT films along both principal directions. The methodology employs two metal lines, one functioning as a heater and the other as a temperature probe, to detect thermal fluctuations and facilitate accurate κ-value extraction. This work offers a comprehensive framework encompassing the measurement principle, fabrication process flow, and experimental design, thereby contributing to a deeper understanding of CNTs’ anisotropic thermal characteristics and their applicability in advanced microelectronic systems.

title: SAR-LiteDETR: A Lightweight Detector for Small Ships in SAR Images
authors: Zhen Yang, Haowei Chen, Zhijian Yin, Xin Zhou, Lijun Deng
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: Small ship detection in synthetic aperture radar (SAR) imagery is challenged by speckle noise, strong scale variation, and complex sea clutter, especially under practical efficiency requirements for SAR-based maritime surveillance. We propose SAR-LiteDETR, a lightweight transformer-based detector tailored for maritime SAR scenes. SAR-LiteDETR includes three lightweight plug-and-play modules: PConv-Blocks, an Attention-Guided Feature Pyramid Network (AG-FPN), and a Channel-wise Multi-Branch Fusion Block (CMBF). These three modules improve backbone efficiency and small-target sensitivity, stabilize multi-scale feature alignment under clutter, and adaptively fuse cross-level features to suppress sea clutter and alleviate feature dilution, respectively, while keeping computational cost low.
