Experiments on the SSDD, HRSID, and LS-SSDD datasets show that SAR-LiteDETR consistently outperforms state-of-the-art and baseline detectors. In particular, it achieves <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$AP_{S}$</tex-math></inline-formula> and precision of 69.6%/97.0% on SSDD and 54.2%/93.5% on HRSID, respectively, while requiring only 42.1 GFLOPs. The results indicate that SAR-LiteDETR provides a favorable balance between detection accuracy and efficiency for near real-time SAR-based maritime surveillance.

title: Learn to Solve Constrained Routing Problems in V2V Social Vehicle Networks
authors: Wei He, Junliang Ye, Lijun Wang, Xiaohu Ge, Tao Han
publish: IEEE Open Journal of the Communications Society
abstract: In vehicle social networks (VSN), multi-hop vehicle-to-vehicle (V2V) routing must minimize end-to-end delay while satisfying a social trust budget. This trust-constrained routing can be formulated as an NP-hard resource-constrained shortest path problem (RCSPP), while the per-link delay is analytically intractable because the global co-channel interference is unknown. To this end, this paper proposes a proactive infeasibility pruning (PIP) mechanism within a constrained Markov decision process (CMDP) framework. PIP constructs a budget-consistent action mask from a shortest-path-to-go prior, providing a conditional guarantee that any policy respecting the mask produces trust-feasible paths. The empirical conditioning rate of this guarantee matches the feasibility ceiling of an exact label-setting (LS) oracle to within 0.8 percentage points across the full trust-budget range. A graph neural network delay surrogate learns the interference-inclusive delay from local observations. System-level evaluation on the Wuhan Optics Valley, China, traffic demonstrates that the proposed method approaches the routing feasibility upper bound and delivers 5.3× more packets than the deep reinforcement learning baselines using only local one-hop information.

title: Context Meets Modality: Deep Learning Innovations for Next-Generation Recommendation Systems
authors: D.Venkata Subramanian, Bhuvan Unhelkar, S Ramacharan, Prasun Chakrabarti
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Emerging Computing and Intelligent Technologies (ICoECIT)
abstract: The complexity and preferences for users have increased exponentially due to the growth and multi-modality of digital content. Users in the modern world typically deal with multiple data types, including video, audio, text, and images, which are influenced by time, place, and society. The existing techniques, namely collaborative filtering and other methods, were not effective due to the sparsity of data and also the partial awareness of the context. To avoid these drawbacks, it is essential to consider multimodal deep learning-based methods to gain awareness of the given context. New architecture models using Multi-modal approaches aim to handle any type of data sources and also based on personalized preferences of users. The developments in attention mechanisms, large language models, transformers, and graph neural networks provide opportunities for dealing with any domain and enable proper synthesis of semi-structured, structured, and unstructured data. This article aims to explore all the research works and innovations carried out in fusion models, attention, graphs, agentic models, and other related developments. In addition to the developments in the frameworks, this paper also gathers information about the explainability, trustworthiness, and fairness of the works carried out by the researchers. The third part of this work is to highlight the summary of the methods and future directions for the frameworks that can support real-time reasoning with fairness so that multimodal data can be handled along with plain text.

title: GAITGen: Disentangled Motion-Pathology Impaired Gait Generative Model – Bringing Motion Generation to the Clinical Domain
authors: Vida Adeli, Soroush Mehraban, Majid Mirmehdi, Alan Whone, Benjamin Filtjens, Amirhossein Dadashzadeh, Alfonso Fasano, Andrea Iaboni, Babak Taati
publish: 2026 IEEE/CVF Winter Conference on Applications of Computer Vision (WACV)
abstract: Gait analysis is crucial for the diagnosis and monitoring of movement disorders like Parkinson’s Disease. While computer vision models have shown potential for objectively evaluating parkinsonian gait, their effectiveness is limited by scarce clinical datasets and challenges in collecting large and well-labelled data, impacting model accuracy and risk of bias. To address these gaps, we propose GAITGen, a novel framework that generates realistic gait sequences conditioned on specified pathology severity levels. GAITGen employs a Conditional Residual Vector Quantized Variational Autoencoder to learn disentangled representations of motion dynamics and pathology-specific factors, coupled with Mask and Residual Transformers for conditioned sequence generation. GAITGen generates realistic, diverse gait sequences across severity levels, enriching datasets and enabling large-scale model training in parkinsonian gait analysis. Experiments on our new PD-GaM (real) dataset demonstrate that GAITGen outperforms adapted state-of-the-art models in both reconstruction fidelity and generation quality, accurately capturing critical pathology-specific gait features. A clinical user study confirms the realism and clinical relevance of our generated sequences. Moreover, incorporating GAITGengenerated data into downstream tasks improves parkinsonian gait severity estimation, highlighting its potential for advancing clinical gait analysis.

title:
Study on a TM
                    <sub>220</sub>
                    ModeOutput Cavity for X-Band Multibeam Klystron and Its ModeStability
authors: Yuan Liang, Honghong Gu, Bin Shen, Dengfeng Lu, Xin Guo, Haibing Ding
abstract: This article focuses on the design and stability study of the TM<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${}_{{220}}~2\pi $</tex-math> </inline-formula>-mode of the double-gap output cavity of the multiple-beam klystron, which consists of the four fundamental mode subcavities with seven beam-tunnels inside each of them. First, based on the 3-D electromagnetic simulation, the basic designs of the output cavity, mode converter, and output system were completed, and the specific cold test method for the gap impedance was provided. To address the complex mode oscillation problem introduced by the high-order mode scheme, combined with particle-in-cell (PIC) simulation calculations, an absorber combination scheme was proposed to suppress or attenuate the oscillation modes. The klystron integrated with this output circuit was tested. During the hot testing, the stable power output and on-site spectral data demonstrated the effectiveness of the output cavity’s mode stability design.

title:
pTree: Building Efficient B
                    <sup>+</sup>
                    -Tree on Non-volatile Memory with Processing-in-memory
authors: Yibo Liu, Bing Wu, Shiyi Song, Xueliang Wei, Huan Cheng, Heng Zhou, Wei Tong, Dan Feng
abstract: B+-Trees are widely used in storage systems and diverse applications. However, their performance is hindered by frequent memory accesses required for key comparisons and structural maintenance. The limited parallelism of CPUs, along with their sensitivity to data order and volume, further restricts the efficiency of B+-Trees. Processing-in-memory (PIM) offers a promising alternative with its in-situ parallel computing capability. In this paper, we propose pTree, a novel set of PIM techniques and architecture specifically designed to accelerate B+-Tree operations. pTree introduces in-situ parallel comparison mechanisms that significantly reduce costly memory accesses and inefficient CPU-side comparisons during tree traversal. This parallelism eliminates the need to maintain intra-node key order and, together with our in-situ parallel node bisecting techniques, greatly minimizes tree structure maintenance overhead during insertions and deletions. Additionally, pTree decouples both comparison and modification efficiency from node size, enabling the use of larger nodes to reduce tree height and traversal complexity. Evaluation shows that pTree reduces the average latency to 35%, 26%, 52%, and 32% compared to state-of-theart B+-Trees for Insert, Search, Update, and Delete operations.

title: Distilling What and Why: Enhancing Driver Intention Prediction with MLLMs
authors: Sainithin Artham, Avijit Dasgupta, Shankar Gangisetty, C. V. Jawahar
publish: 2026 IEEE/CVF Winter Conference on Applications of Computer Vision (WACV)
abstract: Predicting a drivers’ intent (e.g., turns, lane changes) is a critical capability for modern Advanced Driver Assistance Systems (ADAS). While recent Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) show promise in general vision-language tasks, we find that zero-shot MLLMs still lag behind domain-specific approaches for Driver Intention Prediction (DIP). To address this, we introduce DriveXplain, a zero-shot framework based on MLLMs that leverages rich visual cues such as optical flow and road semantics to automatically generate both intention maneuver (what) and rich natural language explanations (why). These maneuver–explanation pairs are then distilled into a compact MLLM, which jointly learns to predict intentions and corresponding explanations. We show that incorporating explanations during training leads to substantial gains over models trained solely on labels, as distilling explanations instills reasoning capabilities by enabling the model to understand not only what decisions to make but also why those decisions are made. Comprehensive experiments across structured (Brain4Cars, AIDE) and unstructured (DAAD) datasets demonstrate that our approach achieves state-of-the-art results in DIP task, outperforming zero-shot and domain-specific baselines. We also present ablation studies to evaluate key design choices in our framework. This work sets a direction for more explainable and generalizable intention prediction in autonomous driving systems. Project webpage: https://avijit9.github.io/DriveXplain/

title: SMS-EMOA-Driven Multiobjective Design-Technology Co-Optimization for 5-nm Gate-All-Around Transistors
authors: Hongwei Yao, Xinyu Zou, Xingyu Shang, Jianing Zhang, Xiaojin Li, Yang Shen, Ziyu Liu, Bingyi Ye, Yuhang Zhang, Yanling Shi, Yabin Sun
abstract: Due to the complexity of physical effects and second-order effects, optimizing the electrical properties of sub-5-nm devices is exceptionally challenging. This article presents an S-Metric Selection Evolutionary Multiobjective Optimization Algorithm (SMS-EMOA)-driven Design-Technology Co-Optimization (DTCO) framework. By uniquely integrating a lightweight performance prediction surrogate model, it efficiently identifies Pareto fronts under strict manufacturing constraints. This achieves a <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$62\times $</tex-math> </inline-formula> speedup and reduces peak memory usage from 12GB to ~200 MB compared to direct technology computer-aided design (TCAD). Furthermore, artificial neural network (ANN)-based compact models can be automatically generated from <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$I$</tex-math> </inline-formula>–<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$V$</tex-math> </inline-formula> and <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$C$</tex-math> </inline-formula>–<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$V$</tex-math> </inline-formula> data for fast circuit-level verification. Considering back-end interconnect parasitics, DTCO is performed on a layout-aware 97-stage NAND2 ring oscillator. Compared with random baselines, the optimized device achieves up to 34% lower power and 65% higher performance, while delivering a > 25% improvement in SRAM Static Noise Margin (SNM) for robust stability. This framework establishes a tight, scalable connection across process, device, layout, and circuit co-optimization.

title: DreamCatcher: Efficient Multi-Concept Customization via Representation Finetuning
authors: Jungwon Lee, Changhun Lee, Eunhyeok Park
publish: 2026 IEEE/CVF Winter Conference on Applications of Computer Vision (WACV)
abstract: Recent advances in customizing Text-to-Image models allow users to generate personalized images with just a few samples. As demand for multi-concept generation grows, methods using weight fusion and test-time optimization have emerged, integrating multiple concepts within a single image. However, these approaches inject concept knowledge into the parametric space, leading to high overhead in multi-concept generation. We introduce DreamCatcher, an efficient framework based on representation finetuning. Our key innovation embeds conceptual information into the feature space, achieving up to 5× faster multi-concept generation while reducing learnable storage per concept by 88%, all without quality loss. Besides, our method is highly versatile, enabling personalized inpainting without additional training.

title: Bridging the Domain Gap in Small Multimodal Models: A Dual-level Alignment Perspective
authors: Aveen Dayal, Peketi Divya, Nidhi Tiwari, Linga Reddy Cenkeramaddi, C Krishna Mohan, Abhinav Kumar
publish: 2026 IEEE/CVF Winter Conference on Applications of Computer Vision (WACV)
abstract: Small Multimodal Models (SMMs) suffer under distribution shift after fine-tuning. Unsupervised Domain Adaptation (UDA) is a common remedy for this issue, but existing theory and methods are designed primarily for single-or dual-encoder architectures, overlooking the encoder–decoder structure of SMMs, whose fusion mechanism introduces additional shift. This work bridges this gap in two steps. First, we derive a dual-divergence risk bound that separates encoder divergence from fusion divergence and illustrate its tightness compared to the classical encoder-only bound with a negation-flip example. Second, motivated by this theory, we propose Dual-level Adversarial Alignment (DuAA), a two-stage alignment algorithm. DuAA inserts domain-discriminative adapters after the encoder and within the decoder to minimize both divergences. Furthermore, DuAA employs selective pseudo-labeling to refine target semantics. Our contribution targets domain shift in encoder–decoder SMMs and is agnostic to the fine-tuning mechanism, i.e., DuAA acts on internal representations, making it orthogonal to LoRA, or other fine-tuning variants. We adopt LoRA in experiments solely as a popular, parameter-efficient instantiation to keep the protocol fixed across settings. We compile twelve new cross-domain VQA tasks with distinct visual and textual shifts from existing datasets and observe that DuAA consistently outperforms standard fine-tuning across all tasks.

title: AaSP: Aliasing-aware Self-Supervised Pre-Training for Audio Spectrogram Transformers
authors: Kohei Yamamoto, Kosuke Okusa
publish: IEEE Transactions on Audio, Speech and Language Processing
abstract: Transformer-based audio self-supervised learning (SSL) models often operate on spectrogram-based time-frequency representations and apply vision-style Transformer architectures (e.g., ViT-style) together with masked modeling objectives (e.g., MAE/MIM-style). Typically, they use convolutional patchification with temporal downsampling. This lowers the effective Nyquist frequency and introduces aliasing, while naïve low-pass filtering removes task-relevant high-frequency cues. In this study, we present AaSP, an aliasing-aware self-supervised pre-training framework for audio spectrogram transformers. AaSP combines an aliasing-aware patch representation, teacher-student masked modeling, a cross-attention predictor, and multi-mask contrastive regularization to learn representations that can better integrate features derived from alias-prone modulation bands while remaining more stable across masked views. Its patch-embedding component, Aliasing-aware Patch Embedding (AaPE), augments standard patch tokens with features derived from alias-prone modulation bands using a band-limited complex sinusoidal kernel with a two-sided exponential window. The kernel's frequency and decay parameters are estimated from the input, enabling adaptive subband analysis whose outputs are fused with the standard patch tokens. These components encourage consistency across masked views, which appears important for realizing the empirical benefit of the aliasing-aware patch representation. We pre-train on AudioSet and evaluate the learned representations via fine-tuning and linear evaluation on downstream benchmarks covering general acoustic/environmental, speech-related, and music-related audio recognition tasks. Under fine-tuning, the full AaSP framework achieves state-of-the-art results on AS-20K, ESC-50, and NSynth among the compared self-supervised baselines, while remaining competitive on the other benchmarks. Linear evaluation shows a broadly consistent trend, with clear improvements on several benchmarks (e.g., US8K and NSynth). Overall, these results suggest that the full AaSP framework can learn representations that are more stable under aliasing-sensitive temporal perturbations and competitive for downstream transfer.

title: Design of Compact Quad-Function Filtering Circuit Using Hybrid Coaxial-Dielectric Resonators
authors: Hui-Yang Li, Kai Yu, Xiaochong Liu, Li Yang, Xiu Yin Zhang
publish: IEEE Microwave and Wireless Technology Letters
abstract: In this letter, we propose a method to design a quad-function filtering circuit using hybrid coaxial-dielectric resonators (DRs). The circuit features the combination of four circuit functions, which can be used to replace a filter, a single-pole double-throw (SPDT) switch, a power divider, and two balun circuits cascading in the radio frequency (RF) front-end system. A circuit model based on coupled resonators is introduced to combine the functions of an SPDT switch and a bandpass filter (BPF), where the switching of the passband is realized by controlling the admittances within the filter structure. Then, by using coaxial resonators, multiple feeding ports are simply arranged to the resonator to implement the circuit function of the power divider. Moreover, balanced-to-unbalanced signal conversion is enabled by utilizing the electromagnetic-field properties of the TM<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">01</sub>-mode DR without increasing the circuit complexity. Compared to conventional designs using cascaded circuits, the proposed circuit shows greatly reduced size and loss. For verification, a prototype circuit is fabricated and measured. Experimental results show good bandpass responses with a wide stopband (for filter), high common-mode (CM) suppression (for balun), accurate power division (for power divider), as well as low loss and high isolation (for SPDT switch).

title: SV3.3B: A Sports Video Understanding Model for Action Recognition
authors: Sai Varun Kodathala, Yashwanth Reddy Vutukoori, Rakesh Vunnam
publish: 2025 Conference on AI, Science, Engineering, and Technology (AIxSET)
abstract: This paper addresses the challenge of automated sports video analysis, which has traditionally been limited by computationally intensive models requiring server-side processing and lacking fine-grained understanding of athletic movements. Current approaches struggle to capture the nuanced biomechanical transitions essential for meaningful sports analysis, often missing critical phases like preparation, execution, and followthrough that occur within seconds. To address these limitations, we introduce SV3.3B, a lightweight <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{3. 3 B}$</tex> parameter video understanding model that combines temporal motion difference sampling with self-supervised learning for efficient on device deployment. Our approach employs a DWT-VGG16-LDA based keyframe extraction mechanism that intelligently identifies the 16 most representative frames from sports sequences, followed by a V-DWT-JEPA2 encoder pretrained through mask-denoising objectives and an LLM decoder fine-tuned for sports action description generation. Evaluated on a subset of the NSVA basketball dataset, SV3.3B achieves superior performance across both traditional text generation metrics and sports-specific evaluation criteria, outperforming larger closed-source models including GPT-4o variants while maintaining significantly lower computational requirements. Our model demonstrates exceptional capability in generating technically detailed and analytically rich sports descriptions, achieving improvement over GPT-4o in ground truth validation metrics, with substantial improvements in information density, action complexity, and measurement precision metrics essential for comprehensive athletic analysis.

title: Revolutionizing Literary Sentiment Analysis with AI A Novel Deep Learning Approach
authors: Munawar Yusuf Sayed, Yashwant Waykar, R. Subhashini, Kalpanapriyadharshini Thiyagarajan, Ramesh Vijaya Babu K, B Kiran Bala
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on AI Engineering and Innovations (AIEI)
abstract: Artificial intelligence revolution in literary analysis is casually put in here, and keeping in mind the rather frivolous nature of the comment, sentiment is extracted from some rather complex text structures. In view of linguistic impediments and contextual depth of meaning, as well as authorial intent, we will report on a new framework for sentiment analysis of text or literature, powered by AI. While normal sentiment analysis models seem to fail in deciphering literary ambiguity, we use a combination of deep learning and NLP techniques so that transformer-based architectures could be used to improve sentiment accuracy. The hybrid setting of the new research stands for semantic embedding features and affective computing concepts which can identify nuanced emotions in literature. The second aspect enhancing our model relative to other existing sentiment analysis tools is the inclusion of the domain-adaptive type of training, thereby enhancing understanding of the context of literary expressions. Additionally, is the new metric called sentiment impact that measures the emotional engagement of readers, which will produce insights into appreciation of literature and psychology of the reader. The framework will be transparent and simple so that it will be clear in terms of methodology and application. Such work will have enormous consequences for the criticism of literature, automated textual interpretation, and digital humanities, truly marking a new era of precision in the undertaking of AI-assisted literary analysis. Bridging the worlds of computational intelligence and the lay realm of interpretation for sentiment in literature, our framework creates possibilities for exploring the emotional depth of textual art forms.

title: Training-Free Few-Shot Segmentation via Vision-Language Guided Prompting
authors: Euihyun Yoon, Taejin Park, Jaekoo Lee
publish: 2026 IEEE/CVF Winter Conference on Applications of Computer Vision (WACV)
abstract: Object segmentation relies heavily on costly pixel-level annotations and struggles to generalize to unseen domains. The recent introduction of the Segment Anything Model (SAM), a foundation model for segmentation, offers a prompt-driven, zero-shot capability that has been applied in various domains (e.g., autonomous driving, satellite imagery, medical imaging) and extended to Few-Shot Segmentation (FSS) tasks. However, existing SAM-based FSS methods typically generate prompts by using a vision encoder to measure support–query image similarity, which often biases towards the support images and fails when there are significant support–query context shifts. To address this limitation, we propose a training-free FSS approach that combines visual and textual cues to generate effective prompts for the target class. By leveraging both vision and language information, our approach bridges the support–query gap and guides SAM to segment novel objects more reliably. Without any additional training, our method outperforms previous state-of-the-art FSS methods on established benchmarks (COCO-20<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">i</sup>, Pascal-5<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">i</sup>), demonstrating its effectiveness and robust generalization. Our code is publicly available on GitHub.

title: ENCORE: A Neural Collapse Perspective on Out-of-Distribution Detection in Deep Neural Networks
authors: A.Q.M. Sazzad Sayyed, Nathaniel D. Bastian, Francesco Restuccia
publish: 2026 IEEE/CVF Winter Conference on Applications of Computer Vision (WACV)
abstract: Out-of-Distribution (OOD) detection is of paramount importance in guaranteeing safe and reliable deployment of a Deep Neural Network (DNN) model in real-world settings. However, most OOD detection approaches still lack motivation rooted in established properties of the DNNs. This disconnect between the proposed approach and theoretical underpinning to measurable DNN properties makes these approaches unreliable. To bridge this gap, we take a different perspective to using energy scoring for OOD detection. Specifically, we look at energy score through the lens of the properties of neural collapse and observe that simple feature scaling can improve the separation between In-Distribution (ID) and OOD inputs. Based on this observation, we propose ENCORE, which scales features of each input adaptively and uses them to obtain modified logits based on insights from theory of neural collapse. We show that ENCORE outperforms state-of-the-art approaches – for example, by 1.37% on CIFAR10 and by 1.07% on Imagenet benchmarks. We are sharing the implementation code<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">1</sup>.

title: AI-Powered Incident Response Framework for Secure DevOps Environments
authors: Santosh Datta Bompally
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Emerging Computing and Intelligent Technologies (ICoECIT)
abstract: Contemporary DevOps systems are also under constant pressure to keep their systems reliable, to identify anomalies and to react to security incidents on the fly. The nature of infrastructure and the pace of deployment render manual incident response ineffective and fraught as organizations become more dependent and more reliant on constant integration and delivery pipelines. The current solutions, including Deep Q-Network (DQN), Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) and the random forest-based models, have been implemented in incident prediction and management, and face limitations related to the lack of ability to perform the predictions across a variety of different operational contexts, sluggishness in reaching the convergence and the low level of the adaptability to evolving security threats. In order to address these issues, this paper will introduce an AI-Powered Incident Response Framework of Secure DevOps Environments (AIRF-SD) to predict, prioritize, and mitigate incidents using reinforcement learning and intelligent automation. The suggested model puts forward the incident response process as a Markov Decision Process (MDP) and uses a reward based Q-learning approach to dynamically optimalize decisions in accordance with operational condition. It is tested on a synthesized dataset of DevOps incidents and the performance of the system is compared with baseline models such as DQN, PPO and Random Forest. It has been shown that AIRF-SD has an accuracy of 96.4%, precision of 0.95, recall of 0.96, and an AUC of 0.97, and is faster to converge and less time consuming to execute than any baseline. These deliverables affirm the efficiency of the suggested framework in proactive, adaptive, and reliable incident response of secure DevOps operations.

title: Exploiting Label-Independent Regularization from Spatial Patterns for Whole Slide Image Analysis
authors: Weiyi Wu, Xinwen Xu, Chongyang Gao, Xingjian Diao, Siting Li, Jiang Gui
publish: 2026 IEEE/CVF Winter Conference on Applications of Computer Vision (WACV)
abstract: Whole slide images, with their gigapixel-scale panoramas of tissue samples, are pivotal for precise disease diagnosis. However, their analysis is hindered by immense data size and scarce annotations. Existing MIL methods face challenges due to the fundamental imbalance where a single bag-level label must guide the learning of numerous patch-level features. This sparse supervision makes it difficult to reliably identify discriminative patches during training, leading to unstable optimization and suboptimal solutions. We propose a spatially regularized MIL framework that leverages inherent spatial relationships among patch features as label-independent regularization signals. Our approach learns a shared representation space by jointly optimizing feature-induced spatial reconstruction and label-guided classification objectives, enforcing consistency between intrinsic structural patterns and supervisory signals. Experimental results on multiple public datasets demonstrate significant improvements over state-of-the-art methods, offering a promising direction. The code is available at https://github.com/wwyi1828/SRMIL.

title: AI-Assisted Breast Cancer Diagnosis Using Vision Transformer
authors: M. Rajasekaran, Ashok Reddy Polaka, Karthik Reddy P
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on AI Engineering and Innovations (AIEI)
abstract: Breast cancer remains one of most prevalent causes cancers among women, and it’s widely spreading all over the world, with the largest number of cases being recorded. So early detection can improve treatment and get better results. Deep learning models consist of quality and promising medical image analysis, which helps to obtain a deep analysis of the disease by using convolutional neural networks, which have a limited modeling range and long-range dependencies, which are very important for detecting the lesions in the images. So, this paper presents a real-time AI-assisted diagnostic framework. It helps to combine a vision transformer encoder with a U-Net-inspired decoder for accurate tumor detection and segmentation. The vision transformer encoder separates the medical image and patches up and employs self-attention to model and global dependencies, while the decoder goes back to reconstruct detailed segmentation of the maps and gives precise localization of tumor regions. So this system is deployed on an edge AI platform to get low latency interface directly at point of care, preserving patient privacy by getting data locally and explainable AI model helps to get attention-based heat maps to know model predictions, improving clinical trust by doing experiment and using a hybrid mammogram ultrasound dataset, we can explain the effectiveness of the model by combining transformer-based global feature extraction, privacy-preserving edge deployment and visual interoperability. This proposed framework gives a better and reliable solution for real-time breast cancer diagnosis.

title:
Comparative Analysis and Optimization of Digital CR-RC
                    <sup>n</sup>
                    Pulse Shaping Algorithms for
                    <sup>3</sup>
                    He Neutron Detectors
authors: Jae Chang Kim, Junehyung Lee Bernaski, Gyuhyeon Sim, Sehwan Seol, Yong Kyun Kim
abstract: Precise neutron signal discrimination and timing measurements are critical in nuclear material analysis systems utilizing Differential Die-Away (DDA) techniques, particularly under high-count-rate conditions. In this study, to ensure economic efficiency and design flexibility, a list-mode based digital data acquisition system was established by directly digitizing the preamplifier output signals of <sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sup>He proportional counters, bypassing conventional analog signal processing units such as shaping amplifiers. To mitigate the pile-up effects of preamplifier signals, CR-RC<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">n</sup> recursive algorithms—well-suited for real-time processing—were introduced; however, systematic research on performance variations across different derivation methods and their specific suitability for neutron measurement environments remains insufficient. To address this, the algorithm proposed in this study (Case 1) and three representative existing algorithms (Case 2: Nakhostin, Case 3: Liu, and Case 4: Hong) were selected and comparatively verified through Monte Carlo simulations and neutron generator experiments. The performance was evaluated using seven metrics: universal parameters such as noise suppression, computational cost, and ballistic deficit, as well as specialized indicators essential for <sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sup>He neutron detection, including discrimination margin, full-energy peak resolution, pile-up resistance, and sampling frequency robustness. The results demonstrated that Case 2 is unsuitable for high-count-rate environments due to baseline undershoot caused by the absence of a pole-zero cancellation function. Case 4, despite its relatively superior resistance to ballistic deficit, faced limitations in real-time measurement due to excessive computation time (approximately four times longer than other algorithms) resulting from its serial processing structure. In contrast, while Case 1 and Case 3 exhibited comparable overall performance, Case 1 demonstrated overwhelming stability in sampling frequency robustness. Given that this provides a significant practical advantage by reducing the burden of recalibration caused by sampling variations during DDA system optimization, Case 1 was selected as the final algorithm for the general-purpose CPU-based DDA system in this study.

title:
Linear V
                    <sub>FB</sub>
                    Tuning and Interface Improvement via TMA/Ultrathin HfO
                    <sub>2</sub>
                    Dual-Buffer in La
                    <sub>2</sub>
                    O
                    <sub>3</sub>
                    Dipole-First Gate Stacks
authors: Wenjun Zhang, Jing Xu, Keyou Tong, Sirong Liu, Shuai Yang, Wenjuan Xiong, Binbin Xu, Yong Du, Xiaoxue Ji, Jianhong Liu, Jianfeng Gao, Xiaolei Wang, Jun Luo
abstract: In this work, a novel dual-buffer approach combining trimethylaluminum (TMA) pretreatment with an ultrathin HfO<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub> buffer layer is proposed to achieve linear flat-band voltage (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${V}_{\text {FB}}$</tex-math> </inline-formula>) modulation, while suppressing La-Si interdiffusion in La<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>O<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sub> dipole-first gate stacks. Specifically, the Al-dipoles introduced by TMA pretreatment reduce the strength of La-induced dipoles, while the ultrathin HfO<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub> buffer further weakens the La-dipoles at the physical level and suppresses La-Si interdiffusion. By systematically optimizing the HfO<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub> buffer thickness, a three-cycle HfO<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub> buffer is identified as the optimal configuration, which minimizes both trap/detrap electron density (N<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">ot</sub>) and interface-state density (D<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">it</sub>) with an equivalent oxide thickness (EOT) penalty of merely 0.3Å. Using this approach, eight distinct <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${V}_{\text {FB}}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> levels are achieved, with a linear step of ~36 mV per cycle over a total range of 290mV, while keeping the maximum modulation within 370 mV. In addition, the Si/SiO<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub> interface quality is significantly improved, with N<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">ot</sub> and D<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">it</sub> reduced by 51%–56% and 40%–64%, respectively. Furthermore, the negative bias stress (NBS) stability is also improved by the TMA/HfO<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub> dual-buffer. Therefore, the TMA/HfO<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub> dual-buffer approach provides a promising and efficient threshold voltage (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${V}_{\text {T}}$</tex-math> </inline-formula>) tuning strategy for state-of-the-art nanosheet gate-all-around field-effect transistors (NS GAA-FETs).

title: SegMo: Segment-aligned Text to 3D Human Motion Generation
authors: Bowen Dang, Lin Wu, Xiaohang Yang, Zheng Yuan, Zhixiang Chen
publish: 2026 IEEE/CVF Winter Conference on Applications of Computer Vision (WACV)
abstract: Generating 3D human motions from textual descriptions is an important research problem with broad applications in video games, virtual reality, and augmented reality. Recent methods align the textual description with human motion at the sequence level, neglecting the internal semantic structure of modalities. However, both motion descriptions and motion sequences can be naturally decomposed into smaller and semantically coherent segments, which can serve as atomic alignment units to achieve finer-grained correspondence. Motivated by this, we propose SegMo, a novel Segment-aligned text-conditioned human Motion generation framework to achieve fine-grained text–motion alignment. Our framework consists of three modules: (1) Text Segment Extraction, which decomposes complex textual descriptions into temporally ordered phrases, each representing a simple atomic action; (2) Motion Segment Extraction, which partitions complete motion sequences into corresponding motion segments; and (3) Fine-grained Text–Motion Alignment, which aligns text and motion segments with contrastive learning. Extensive experiments demonstrate that SegMo improves the strong baseline on two widely used datasets, achieving an improved TOP 1 score of 0.553 on the HumanML3D test set. Moreover, thanks to the learned shared embedding space for text and motion segments, SegMo can also be applied to retrieval-style tasks such as motion grounding and motion-to-text retrieval.

title: Distilling Large Language Models for Network Active Queue Management_supp1-3690076.pdf
authors: Shiva Raj Pokhrel
abstract: We propose AQM-LLM, a framework that distills Large Language Models for Active Queue Management in modern networks. Unlike conventional learning-based AQMs that require extensive feature engineering and struggle with dynamic conditions, our approach leverages the contextual reasoning capability of LLMs to enhance the Low Latency, Low Loss, and Scalable Throughput (L4S) architecture with minimal manual intervention. The L4S-LLM design introduces three key components: (i) a state encoder that transforms heterogeneous network telemetry into token embeddings, (ii) a specialized L4S-LLM head that produces congestion actions in a single inference step, and (iii) a data-driven Low-Rank Adaptation scheme that drastically reduces trainable parameters while preserving accuracy. Our open-source FreeBSD-14 implementation demonstrates improved queue delay stability and higher bandwidth utilization for both DCTCP and UDP Prague traffic. We emphasize that this work demonstrates architectural feasibility through controlled experiments; deployment on router-class hardware will require additional model optimization (e.g., compression, pruning, or quantization) and is left for future work.

title: A Multiport DC Circuit Breaker With N-1 Security Ability
authors: Yanxun Guo, Tongxin Zhao, Xiaomei Yao, Haifeng Li, Wei Han
abstract: In DC grids, multiple DC circuit breakers (DCCBs) on a DC bus can be replaced by a multiport DCCB (MDCCB) to reduce the investment cost. However, compared with a single DCCB, the MDCCB contains more branches and has a greater probability of branch unavailability; furthermore, its interruption failure caused by branch unavailability leads to more severe consequences. A few studies avoid the interruption failure caused by ultrafast disconnector (UFD) failure, but the interruption failure caused by the semiconductor branch unavailability is rarely considered. This article proposes a novel MDCCB with N-1 security ability, which is applicable for different numbers of ports and can interrupt faults even when any internal branch is unavailable. The working principles under normal condition and branch unavailability conditions are presented. The fault currents of DC grids are calculated to design the parameters of MDCCB. The feasibility of the proposed topology is validated by a small-scale prototype, and the working performance is further validated by simulations in the DC grid. Compared with existing MDCCBs, the proposed MDCCB combines the characteristics of low investment cost and high reliability, which can ensure the safety and economy of DC grids.

title: Geometry-Aware Scene Configurations for Novel View Synthesis
authors: Minkwan Kim, Changwoon Choi, Young Min Kim
publish: IEEE Transactions on Visualization and Computer Graphics
abstract: We propose scene-adaptive strategies to efficiently allocate representation capacity for generating immersive experiences of indoor environments from incomplete observations. Indoor scenes with multiple rooms often exhibit irregular layouts with varying complexity, containing clutter, occlusion, and flat walls. We maximize the utilization of limited resources with guidance from geometric priors, which are often readily available after pre-processing stages. We record observation statistics on the estimated geometric scaffold and guide the optimal placement of bases, which greatly improves upon the uniform basis arrangements adopted by previous scalable scene representations. We also suggest scene-adaptive virtual viewpoints to compensate for geometric deficiencies inherent in view configurations in the input trajectory and impose the necessary regularization. We present a comprehensive analysis and discussion regarding rendering quality and memory requirements in several large-scale indoor scenes, demonstrating significant enhancements compared to baselines that employ regular placements.

title: ECG-Deepfusion: Hybrid Temporal and Attention-Based Learning for ECG Classification
authors: Moumita Roy, Sukanta Majumder, Sunipa Roy, Anindya Halder, Utpal Biswas
publish: 2025 2nd International Conference on Emerging Trends in Electronic Devices and Computational Techniques (EDCT)
abstract: Electrocardiogram (ECG) signals pose significant classification challenges due to their non-stationary characteristics and the presence of both short- and long-term temporal dependencies. To tackle these issues, this paper introduces a hybrid deep learning framework that integrates several architectures into a unified model. The framework employs Temporal Convolutional Networks (TCN) with dilated convolutions for multi-scale temporal feature extraction, BiLSTM and BiGRU layers to capture bidirectional sequential dependencies, and a CNN with global average pooling to highlight local discriminative patterns. A multihead attention mechanism is applied to emphasize the most important features, while additional LSTM and GRU layers further refine temporal representations before the final classification. The model is trained and tested on the ECG5000 dataset, which includes five heartbeat classes. Results show that the proposed framework achieved <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{9 8. 4 0 \%}$</tex> accuracy, with precision, recall, and F1-score all at 0.98. Comparison with current state-of-theart methods demonstrates the superior accuracy, robustness, and tighter confidence intervals provided by the proposed approach. These results validate the model's suitability for highperformance ECG classification and its potential as a strong baseline for future time-series analysis research.

title: Anatomy-VLM: A Fine-grained Vision-Language Model for Medical Interpretation
authors: Difei Gu, Yunhe Gao, Mu Zhou, Dimitris Metaxas
publish: 2026 IEEE/CVF Winter Conference on Applications of Computer Vision (WACV)
abstract: Accurate disease interpretation from radiology remains challenging due to imaging heterogeneity. Achieving expert-level diagnostic decisions requires integration of subtle image features with clinical knowledge. Yet major vision-language models (VLMs) treat images as holistic entities and overlook fine-grained image details that are vital for disease diagnosis. Clinicians analyze images by utilizing their prior medical knowledge and identify anatomical structures as important region of interests (ROIs). Inspired from this human-centric workflow, we introduce Anatomy-VLM, a fine-grained, vision-language model that incorporates multi-scale information. First, we design a model encoder to localize key anatomical features from entire medical images. Second, these regions are enriched with structured knowledge for contextually-aware interpretation. Finally, the model encoder aligns multi-scale medical information to generate clinically-interpretable disease prediction. Anatomy-VLM achieves outstanding performance on both in- and out-of-distribution datasets. We also validate the performance of Anatomy-VLM on downstream image segmentation tasks, suggesting that its fine-grained alignment captures anatomical and pathology-related knowledge. Furthermore, the Anatomy-VLM’s encoder facilitates zero-shot anatomy-wise interpretation, providing its strong expert-level clinical interpretation capabilities.

title: Unsupervised Modular Adaptive Region Growing and RegionMix Classification for Wind Turbine Segmentation
authors: Raül Pérez-Gonzalo, Riccardo Magro, Andreas Espersen, Antonio Agudo
publish: 2026 IEEE/CVF Winter Conference on Applications of Computer Vision (WACV)
abstract: Reliable operation of wind turbines requires frequent inspections, as even minor surface damages can degrade aerodynamic performance, reduce energy output, and accelerate blade wear. Central to automating these inspections is the accurate segmentation of turbine blades from visual data. This task is traditionally addressed through dense, pixel-wise deep learning models. However, such methods demand extensive annotated datasets, posing scalability challenges. In this work, we introduce an annotation-efficient segmentation approach that reframes the pixel-level task into a binary region classification problem. Image regions are generated using a fully unsupervised, interpretable Modular Adaptive Region Growing technique, guided by image-specific Adaptive Thresholding and enhanced by a Region Merging process that consolidates fragmented areas into coherent segments. To improve generalization and classification robustness, we introduce RegionMix, an augmentation strategy that synthesizes new training samples by combining distinct regions. Our framework demonstrates state-of-the-art segmentation accuracy and strong cross-site generalization by consistently segmenting turbine blades across distinct windfarms.

title: Power-Efficient Dadda Multiplier Using 9:2 Compressor for Retinal Image Processing
authors: Aditya Bung, Tahir Khan, Arnav Sharma, Ravi S
publish: 2025 2nd International Conference on Emerging Trends in Electronic Devices and Computational Techniques (EDCT)
abstract: This research presents a 8x8 Dadda multiplier that incorporates a novel hierarchical 9:2 compressor, implemented by cascading 5:2 and 4:2 compressors. By integrating the 9:2 compressor, the proposed multiplier significantly reduces the number of partial product stages, resulting in lower power consumption and area. As part of this research, the proposed multiplier is used for medical image processing which focuses mainly on retinal image analysis. Retinal images are very computationally intensive due to the need for precise feature extraction, enhancement, and segmentation for the detection of diseases. The proposed multiplier accelerates these operations by ensuring the precision of Retinal images, which is crucial for the accurate detection of eye diseases. The proposed design is analyzed using 45 nm technology and synthesized using Cadence tools, resulting in up to 42.5% power savings, 35.6% area reduction. The results validate that the proposed multiplier presents a compelling solution for low-power, high-speed VLSI applications such as DSP and biomedical imaging.

title: Design and Performance Assessment of Hybrid Halbach Brushless PM Motor for UAV Applications Considering Torque and Rare-Earth Utilization Efficiency
authors: Vaibhav Bhardwaj, Amit Kumar Jain
publish: 2025 IEEE 4th International Conference on Smart Technologies for Power, Energy and Control (STPEC)
abstract: Brushless Permanent Magnet (PM) motors are widely preferred for Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs) due to their superior torque and power-to-weight ratio. Typically, these motors utilize radially magnetized, arc-shaped rare earth permanent magnets. However, these magnets tend to be costly and often need to be custom-designed to suit different motor dimensions. The paper introduces the design of an outer rotor brushless PM motor featuring a rotor that utilizes a hybrid halbach array composed flat magnets. A comparative analysis is then conducted against a conventional brushless PM motor, focusing on torque generated per unit volume of rare earth magnets, and the results are presented.

title: Quantum Simulation of the Efficiency of the SPAD Detector for Space Applications
authors: Kavita Lalwani, Durgesh Tinker, Munish Gakhar
publish: 2026 International Conference on Next-Gen Quantum and Advanced Computing: Algorithms, Security, and Beyond (NQComp)
abstract: Single-Photon Avalanche Diodes (SPADs) are critical components of emerging quantum communication networks, used to detect single photon. They are placed in satellites for longdistance communication and are susceptible to radiation-induced displacement damage in the space environment. This degrades their performance. In this work, SPAD is simulated in a space environment by introducing a quantum simulation framework that leverages second quantization and Fock space truncation to model the SPAD as a two-level (|g⟩,|e⟩), incorporating radiation damage through Lindblad open system dynamics. This approach differs significantly from conventional TCAD simulations, which rely on semiclassical approximations that fail to capture the discrete quantum statistics of single-photon interactions and the dynamics of trap states. In this work, Photon Detection Efficiency and timing jitter, in ideal and damping environments, are simulated as expectation values.

title: A Market Monitoring Framework for European Broadband Using PPP-Adjusted Segmentation and Transition Tracking
authors: Ivan Jajić
publish: 2026 8th International Symposium on Computational and Business Intelligence (ISCBI)
abstract: This paper proposes a data-driven framework for segmenting European fixed broadband markets using purchasing power parity (PPP)-adjusted prices. The analysis focuses on the least expensive fixed broadband offers in 27 European countries for 2019 and 2023, based on Eurostat data. The aim is to identify stable and changing affordability segments, rather than to analyse any single country in detail. Methodologically, the paper combines descriptive statistics, k-means clustering with internal validation indices, an analysis of cluster transitions and repeatedmeasures inference, complemented by a country fixed-effects model. The results indicate a substantial decline in PPP-adjusted prices and a persistent, yet partially mobile, segmentation of the market into cheaper and more expensive groups of countries. In both clusters, the 2023 centroids are lower than in 2019, while the internal validation indices suggest clearer separation between clusters in the later year. A transition matrix shows that around two-thirds of countries remain in the same cluster, whereas the remaining third move between segments. The paper demonstrates how relatively simple clustering and panel techniques can be effectively integrated into a business intelligence framework for monitoring price-based market segments over time.

title: Lightweight Deep Learning Model for Rice Leaf Disease Detection with Region Highlighting
authors: Shandez Darlene, Stephen William, Alexander Agung Santoso Gunawan
publish: 2026 8th International Symposium on Computational and Business Intelligence (ISCBI)
abstract: Rice is one of the world's most essential crops, yet it is highly vulnerable to leaf diseases that can significantly reduce yield. Conventional visual diagnosis methods are often slow and impractical in rural areas. While recent deep learning approaches offer promising solutions, highly accurate models are typically too large for edge deployment, and lightweight models often lack interpretability. This study proposes a lightweight and explainable deep learning framework for rice leaf disease detection on edge devices based on EfficientNet-B0 integrated with attention mechanisms. Three attention modules—Convolutional Block Attention Module (CBAM), Efficient Channel Attention (ECA), and Coordinate Attention (CoordAttention)—were compared based on parameter overhead, computational complexity, and classification performance. Among them, CoordAttention achieved the best balance across these metrics and was therefore adopted for the final architecture. The proposed CoordEficientNetB0 model was evaluated on a dataset of 5,188 augmented images covering seven disease categories along with healthy leaves, and further assessed using a five-fold crossvalidation strategy. Experimental results show that the model achieved an accuracy of 93.83%, with a compact size of approximately 4.2 million parameters and an 8.5 MB model size after TensorFlow Lite conversion, enabling efficient inference on edge devices. In addition, qualitative visualization results demonstrate that the proposed attention mechanism effectively highlights disease-relevant regions, thereby enhancing model interpretability. Overall, the integration of CoordAttention improves EfficientNet-B0 in both classification accuracy and lesion localization with no significant increase in computational cost.

title: Rethinking Real Image Editing: Unleashing Diverse Editing Operators via Multi-Objective Optimization
authors: Siyuan Wang, Xi Yang, Zihao Zhou, Huiru Shao, Rui Zhang, Qiufeng Wang, Guangliang Cheng, Kaizhu Huang
publish: 2026 IEEE/CVF Winter Conference on Applications of Computer Vision (WACV)
abstract: Text-conditioned diffusion models have revolutionized the field of controllable real image editing, enabling high-fidelity and precise image manipulation. Recent methods target specific editing tasks, using internal representations from reconstruction to ensure consistency. Although effective for single tasks, they fail to balance precision and consistency across diverse image editing tasks. In this work, we propose a novel inference-time real-image editing framework that enables executing multiple editing tasks by tuning editing operators. Our key insight is to treat real image editing as a multi-objective optimization problem, optimizing editing operators for a Pareto optimal solution that balances editing accuracy and consistency at each denoising iteration. Additionally, we design a benchmark for operator-guided real-image editing that covers various local and global editing tasks. Extensive experimental evaluations demonstrate the method’s effectiveness in executing precise edits while preserving image fidelity across all tasks, thereby establishing it as the new state-of-the-art.

title: QUBO Model for Optimization of Crops in Agricultural Lands using Graph Coloring Technique
authors: Madhumathi Ramasamy, Sai Sakunthala Guddanti, Anil Prabhakar
publish: 2026 International Conference on Next-Gen Quantum and Advanced Computing: Algorithms, Security, and Beyond (NQComp)
abstract: Ensuring a sustainable and abundant global food supply is imperative, particularly in the times of escalating problems posed by population growth and climate change. Integrating smart technologies into agriculture offers a means to enhance both the quantity and quality of food production, while safeguarding farmers economic stability. This work focuses on determining optimal crop placement that maximize soil nutrient use and crop yields. By formulating the problem as a graph coloring task, we explore several optimization techniques, comparing classical brute-force and greedy algorithms with simulated annealing and quantum annealing approaches. While brute force yields exact solutions, its computational cost rises exponentially with problem size. In contrast, both simulated annealing and quantum annealing provide robust, efficient solutions, making them practical choices for large-scale crop planning.

title: Federated Neuro-Adaptive Cyber Defense for Zero-Day Attack Detection in Large-Scale IoT Networks
authors: Paddala Yuvateja, Chenna Keshava Matcha
publish: 2026 World Conference on Computational Science and Technology (WcCST)
abstract: The recent proliferation of large-scale Internet of Things (IoT) networks has posed immense cybersecurity threats, particularly regarding detection of zero-day attacks in distributed and privacy-respecting environments. In this paper we present a novel Federated Neuro-Adaptive Cyber Defense framework that combines privacy-preserving federated learning with neuroadaptive deep-learning-based training to achieve improved intrusion detection performance. We introduce an approach that allows decentralized federated model training over different IoT clients, they will never share the raw data with each other, which is unlike traditional centralized static models while achieving a better synergy by dynamically adapting to new attack patterns emerging with time (also known as concept drift). Experiments conducted with the CICIoT2023 dataset show that the proposed framework significantly outperforms state-of-the-art approaches. In particular, it obtains a high detection accuracy of 99.21% and an F1-score of 98.96%, outperforming centralized deep learning, transfer learning, and traditional federated learning methods. This model achieves a zero-day attack detection accuracy of 93.2% with a false positive rate of just 0.6%, further enhancing cyber defense capabilities and precision. The outcome validates the applicability of federated learning along with the neuroadaptive feature tuning in delivering an innovative, privacyaware and scalable security mechanism for next generation IoT cyber security systems.

title: Copyright and Reprint Permission
authors: N/A
publish: 2026 International Conference on Next-Gen Quantum and Advanced Computing: Algorithms, Security, and Beyond (NQComp)

title: LLM Assisted SecOps: A Cognitive Assistant Framework for Cybersecurity Analysts
authors: Harsh Dineshbhai Bakori, Nishanth Kumar Pathi, Shinu Abhi
publish: 2026 International Conference on Next-Gen Quantum and Advanced Computing: Algorithms, Security, and Beyond (NQComp)
abstract: The complexity of cyber threats is rapidly outpacing the capacity of human analysts, creating an urgent need for intelligent augmentation in cybersecurity. As cybercriminals employ increasingly sophisticated tactics, traditional methods of threat detection and response are becoming inadequate. This situation necessitates a paradigm shift in how cybersecurity is approached, particularly through the integration of Artificial Intelligence (AI). Large Language Models (LLMs) can be positioned not as autonomous systems but as cognitive assistants that enhance human analysts’ capabilities throughout the cyber kill chain. By translating vast, unstructured data into actionable intelligence, LLMs can provide contextual understanding, enabling analysts to make faster, more informed decisions and transition from a reactive to a proactive defensive strategy. The primary challenge lies in the overwhelming volume of data and the speed at which cyber threats evolve. The objective of this project was to explore the role of LLMs as cognitive assistants in cybersecurity, focusing on their ability to enhance the efficiency and effectiveness of human analysts. By leveraging LLMs, we aimed to improve threat identification, incident response, and overall cybersecurity posture, ultimately shifting the focus from merely reacting to threats to anticipating and mitigating them proactively. To achieve these objectives, we employed a comprehensive methodology that involved the collection of diverse datasets, including open-source intelligence, threat reports, and historical incident data. The LLM was connected to perform cybersecurity tasks using an MCP server, enabling it to generate insights and recommendations tailored to the needs of human analysts. The results of our project indicate that the integration of LLMs significantly enhances the capabilities of cybersecurity analysts. By providing real-time analysis and contextual understanding, LLMs enable faster triage of alerts and more accurate threat assessments. Our recommendations include the continued development and integration of LLMs into cybersecurity workflows, emphasizing the importance of human oversight and collaboration.

title: Cover Page
authors: N/A
publish: 2026 8th International Symposium on Computational and Business Intelligence (ISCBI)

title: Optimizing Sales Strategies for MSMEs Through Digital Payments in the Digital Era
authors: Permata Rahayu Sampurno, Audio Valentino Himawan Mahendra
publish: 2026 8th International Symposium on Computational and Business Intelligence (ISCBI)
abstract: This study aims to analyze strategies that can be employed to optimize sales for micro, small, and medium enterprises (MSMEs) through the adoption of digital payment systems. The approach used is descriptive qualitative, with data collected through observations and in-depth interviews with MSME operators who have implemented digital payment systems such as QRIS. Data analysis was conducted through processes of collection, reduction, presentation, and conclusion drawing. This research is expected to reveal how digital payments enhance transaction efficiency, expand market reach, and improve customer convenience, ultimately optimizing MSME sales. The findings of this study will provide strategic recommendations to support the development of MSMEs and enhance their competitiveness in the digital era.

title: Deep Learning Approaches for Predicting Attack Vulnerabilities in Quantum-Secure Financial Networks
authors: Saurabh Kakkar, Sathiyaviradhan Janarthanan, Bhulakshmi Makkena, Armaan Kakkar
publish: 2026 International Conference on Emerging Systems and Intelligent Computing (ESIC)
abstract: In the age of quantum communication and 6G, the safety of financial networks has become a more important concern as cyber threats become more sophisticated. This study examines how deep learning models can be used to improve vulnerability detection and prediction in quantum-secure financial settings. Based on the Quantum-Secure 6G Slicing Dataset, which combines the latest encryption methods, including ECC, AES, and QKD, the study employs preprocessing steps— data cleaning, normalizatio, label encoding, and SMOTE-based balancing to ensure optimal model performance. The suggested ANN was compared with other deep learning and machine learning methods, including Decision Trees (DTs), Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs), and Logistic Regression (LR). The results of the experiments were found to be the highest with ANN, the accuracy, 92.25, precision, 89.58, recall, 93.99, and F1-score, 91.73, which indicates that the ANN outperforms the existing models. These findings indicate that the ANN will be a powerful and robust method for detecting fraud and forecasting attack vulnerability in quantum-secure financial networks, as it implies that the ANN is an effective way to capture complex nonlinear dependencies and achieve better generalization and predictive stability.

title: Gradient Boosting for Automatic Epileptic Seizure Detection in EEG Signals: A Comparative Study with Classical Models
authors: Ignacio D. Lezcano, Edgar H. Ayala-Britez, Diego P. Pinto-Roa
publish: 2026 World Conference on Computational Science and Technology (WcCST)
abstract: The automatic detection of epileptic seizures from EEG signals is a clinical and technical necessity for improving monitoring and medical decision-making. The literature has reported on classical machine learning and deep learning approaches, the former being efficient and the latter requiring significant computational resources. In this regard, it is necessary to develop solutions that achieve a good balance between performance and low computational training costs. The objective of this study is to evaluate an ensemble classifier based on Gradient Boosting, comparing it with classic machine learning methods (SVM, KNN, GNB, DT, LDA, and LR) for binary seizure/non-seizure detection on EEG segments in accordance with the technical recommendations of the ILAE. From 10-second segments of raw signals from the CHB-MIT Scalp EEG Database, feature extraction obtained thirty linear temporal features per channel to capture statistical, shape, and dynamic information in the time domain. The models were trained using stratified crossvalidation with optimal hyperparameters. Gradient Boosting achieved the best overall performance, outperforming classical classifiers in all evaluated metrics, with the following results: Accuracy = 0.9793, Precision = 0.9868, Recall = 0.9720, F1-Score = 0.9792, and Specificity (TNR)=0.9866. The results demonstrate that the Gradient Boosting classifier, when tuned and evaluated using stratified procedures, is a promising alternative for the automatic detection of epileptic seizures in EEG signals. These findings support the use of ensemble approaches as a complementary tool in monitoring systems and clinical decision support.

title: Smart Style: AI-Powered Virtual Stylist for Personalized Fashion Recommendations
authors: A S Syed Fiaz, Abdul Mahaboob Fazil, M Abdullah, M Aravindar V, R. Rajmohan
publish: 2026 International Conference on Electronic Systems and Intelligent Computing (ICESIC)
abstract: The proposed work creates an artificial intelligence-driven virtual stylist which revolutionizes the process of purchasing clothes. Through the system users gain access to customized interactions that operate in real-time. People who use the web application can upload their pictures to obtain dressing suggestions that align with their style preferences. The system uses text evaluation along with image processing techniques to assess user clothing alternatives. The system employs computer vision technology to establish a virtual system for clothing tryons. The tool allows users to see their picked clothes in their personal photos before making a purchase. Users can examine different clothing styles based on their body shapes before making any purchase decisions. The shopping experience this product provides feels pleasant for users. The software assists students in developing their self-esteem. Platform users receive smart recommendations, as well as visual demonstrations of their picked items. Users on the platform receive the ability to uncover fresh fashion selections, which they can mix to achieve their personal looks for all types of occasions. A personalized stylist combines modern technology with styling solutions for an almost new shopping experience.

title: Predicting Significant Wave Height using Random Forest with Wind Speed and SST
authors: Aaditi Ghodke, Girija Giri, Manvi Ankalgi, Nitin Sawalkar, Riddhi Mirajkar
publish: 2026 World Conference on Computational Science and Technology (WcCST)
abstract: The Significant Wave Height (SWH) is an essential factor in maritime navigation, port operations and safety of the coastal infrastructure. The correct forecasting of SWH is critical to the reduction of risks of the extreme state of the ocean and ability to control the port environment in a sustainable manner. This paper examines how wind elements (eastwest and northsouth), the speed of wind, and the Sea Surface Temperature (SST) affect SWH in three major Indian ports namely Cochin, Visakhapatnam, and Jawaharlal Nehru Port Trust (JNPT). The data analyzed are based on long-term reanalysis (1979-2009) that is 6 hourly (00, 06, 12, 18 UTC). The relationships between atmospheric and oceanic variables are analyzed using statistical means, correlation, trend, time-series analysis and so on. This is then predicted in a Random Forest machine learning model that effectively excludes linear interactions and regional variability. The findings show that wind speed, wind direction, and SST have strong effects on SWH with a clear difference between the ports. Moreover, an interactive analysis can be obtained with the help of a user-friendly interface created with the help of Streamlit. On the whole, the proposed study combines statistical and machine learning methods to make the prediction of waves more accurate, facilitate the preparedness to coastal hazards, and ensure the safety and efficiency of ports functioning as part of the sustainable coastal management.

title: Next-Generation Time-Series Forecasting: The Shift from Deep Learning to Spiking Neural Networks
authors: Sneha Sasidharan, Bindu P V
publish: 2026 International Conference on Emerging Systems and Intelligent Computing (ESIC)
abstract: Time-series forecasting has gone through several different generations of modeling, from classical statistical tools to neural architectures, each driven by shifts in computational requirements and complexities of real-world data. Early forecasting pipelines relied on statistical and machine learning approaches coping reasonably well with short-term patterns but struggling with nonlinear dynamics or structural shifts. Deep learning models pushed the field forward by capturing longrange dependencies. However, they require substantial amounts of training data, large memory footprints, and high energy consumption, making them impractical in edge environments or on low-power devices. Spiking Neural Networks represent a promising next step in this evolution. They operate on the basis of discrete spikes rather than continuous activations and are substantially different from classical deep learning. Although the supporting ecosystem is still growing, Spiking Neural Networks offer the strong promise of lower power usage, inherent temporal sensitivity arising from the neuron model, and native compatibility with neuromorphic hardware. This paper studies the evolution of time-series prediction from traditional models to deep learning-based forecasting and finally to Spiking Neural Network-driven time-series prediction. It outlines the motivations for each shift, the technical innovations that enabled the progress, and the limitations that pushed researchers toward the next generation of models. By tracing this pathway, this paper seeks to provide a clearer picture of where Spiking Neural Network-based forecasting currently stands and what gaps remain before these biologically inspired models can serve as practical, mainstream tools for time-series prediction.

title: ObjectMeshDeform : Towards recovering precise 3D geometry of real objects via image-guided mesh deformation of 3D generative priors
authors: Siddharth Katageri, Sanjana Sinha, Sourav Ghosh, Soumyadip Maity, Brojeshwar Bhowmick
publish: 2026 IEEE/CVF Winter Conference on Applications of Computer Vision (WACV)
abstract: 3D Generative Models that synthesize high fidelity 3D assets from single view or multi-view images cannot recover precise 3D geometry and dimensions of real-world objects, which is often required by practical applications. On the other hand, multi-view 3D reconstruction methods based on structure from motion, implicit surfaces, gaussian splatting, fail to recover high fidelity object meshes with dense geometry, shape regularity, smooth surfaces present in real world objects. In this paper we propose a novel approach that leverages a 3D mesh prior synthesized by generative models pre-trained on large scale 3D synthetic datasets. Our method automatically refines the 3D geometry of the generated meshes for improved geometric precision using only sparse multi-view input images, while maintaining the geometric fidelity of the generated prior meshes. Our method can automatically reconstruct meshes from images of real world objects, without requiring any additional large-scale training data or manual inputs for shape deformation.

title: Spatio-Temporal Modelling and Ensemble Risk Forecasting of Drug-Trafficking Surveillance Using NDPS Records in India
authors: Jay Chafale, Ankita Avthankar, Nilesh Shelke, Tabassum H. Khan, Harika Vanam, Amit Pimpalkar
publish: 2026 International Conference on Emerging Systems and Intelligent Computing (ESIC)
abstract: The abundance of narcotic substances under India's Narcotic Drugs and Psychotropic Substances (NDPS) Act, 1985, poses a significant challenge to law enforcement and public health. Traditional enforcement strategies are often reactive and lack predictive insight. This paper presents an Interactive GeoAnalytics Dashboard designed to forecast future drug seizure hotspots, enabling proactive resource allocation. The study consolidates official NDPS data from 2018-2020 with validated media-reported seizure data through 2025, creating a robust eightyear dataset. Using advanced data science method-ologies including exploratory data analysis, feature engineering, and predictive modeling the project identifies critical spatiotemporal patterns and forecasts state-level seizure quantities for 2026 using a Random Forest Prophet ensemble. The ensemble achieves an <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{R}^{\mathbf{2}}$</tex> score of 0.87 and an RMSE of 12.4 kg. Findings indicate a rapid rise in synthetic drug seizures and the emergence of new hotspots across the Northeast corridor. The resulting dashboard, built with GeoPandas, Plotly, and Streamlit, provides actionable intelligence for NDPS agencies, marking a shift from reactive to data-driven enforcement.

title: A Performance-Centric Investigation of ICT–Enabled Information Integration and Decision Support in Healthcare Supply Chain Systems
authors: S. Surendharan, M Krithika
publish: 2026 International Conference on Electronic Systems and Intelligent Computing (ICESIC)
abstract: The adoption of ICT to real-time secure and performance-based operations of the supply chain is urgent in an age of growing complexity in healthcare logistics. The proposed paper introduces an innovative framework that uses rare, yet, powerful computational methods, such as the Rough Set Theory, the Dempser-Shafer Theory, the Complex Event Processing (CEP), the Blockchain-Graph combination, and the Federated Learning, to increase hospital supply network visibility, trust, and predictive intelligence. To integrate heterogeneous data of IoT, EHRs and supplier systems, a multi-tiered architecture with semantic interoperability layers was created. The research findings indicated that CEP-based event detection accuracy was 95.5%, federated demand prediction accuracy was 92.1% and supplier trust estimation accuracy was more than 93% with blockchain-supported audit trails. Simulations of Digital Twin were also used to confirm the resilience of the system in the face of such disruptions as pandemics and cyberattacks. The suggested model was superior to the traditional ERP and monolithic analytics systems by providing context-sensitive choices to the privacyconscious intelligence. The results of this paper highlight the promise of infrequent ICT practices in changing the healthcare logistics into an at-will-you-need-it system instead of a have-you-need-it system. The framework is an interoperable, scalable and performance-focused solution to next-generation healthcare supply chains, which provides continuity, compliance, and cost-effectiveness in mission-critical situations.

title: Generative AI with MCP for Contextual Persona Generation and Customer Engagement
authors: Vinay Mehra, Alok Tibrewala
abstract: When it comes to personalized and empathy-driven customer service in the travel industry, there is an ongoing difficulty in aggregating data from fragmented sources and imposing too much cognitive load on agents who need to sift through a customer's detailed history while they are responding in real time. In this paper, we introduce a novel framework that combines the Model Context Protocol (MCP) capabilities with Generative AI (GenAI) to streamline and enhance customer service interactions. The system unifies disparate data—such as voice call histories, chat transcripts, and booking records—into a single, accessible customer profile. As soon as the customer is engaged, the GenAI element uses MCP to access and review historical data, performing sentiment analysis on recent work, and creates a contextual snapshot that highlights major pain points, previous resolutions, and noteworthy positive experiences. This summary is presented in real time to agents with sentiment scores and a persona through which customers are classified as friendly, aggressive, loyal, conservative, discount seeker, or attentive to tailor a more personalized and empathetic engagement.
